ABSTRACT
We introduce Zep, a novel memory layer service for AI agents that outperforms the current stateof-the-art system, MemGPT, in the Deep Memory Retrieval (DMR) benchmark. Additionally, Zep
excels in more comprehensive and challenging evaluations than DMR that better reflect real-world
enterprise use cases. While existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) frameworks for large
language model (LLM)-based agents are limited to static document retrieval, enterprise applications
demand dynamic knowledge integration from diverse sources including ongoing conversations and
business data. Zep addresses this fundamental limitation through its core component Graphiti—a
temporally-aware knowledge graph engine that dynamically synthesizes both unstructured conversational data and structured business data while maintaining historical relationships. In the DMR
benchmark, which the MemGPT team established as their primary evaluation metric, Zep demonstrates superior performance (94.8% vs 93.4%). Beyond DMR, Zep’s capabilities are further validated through the more challenging LongMemEval benchmark, which better reflects enterprise use
cases through complex temporal reasoning tasks. In this evaluation, Zep achieves substantial results
with accuracy improvements of up to 18.5% while simultaneously reducing response latency by
90% compared to baseline implementations. These results are particularly pronounced in enterprisecritical tasks such as cross-session information synthesis and long-term context maintenance, demonstrating Zep’s effectiveness for deployment in real-world applications.
1 Introduction
The impact of transformer-based large language models (LLMs) on industry and research communities has garnered
siant attention in recent years [1]. A major application of LLMs has been the development of chat-based agents.
However, these agents’ capabilities are limited by the LLMs’ context windows, effective context utilization, and
knowledge gained during pre-training. Consequently, additional context is required to provide out-of-domain (OOD)
knowledge and reduce hallucinations.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a key area of interest in LLM-based applications. RAG
leverages Information Retrieval (IR) techniques pioneered over the last fifty years[2] to supply necessary domain
knowledge to LLMs.
Current approaches using RAG have focused on broad domain knowledge and largely static corpora—that is, document contents added to a corpus seldom change. For agents to become pervasive in our daily lives, autonomously
solving problems from trivial to highly complex, they will need access to a large corpus of continuously evolving data
from users’ interactions with the agent, along with related busd world data. We view empowering agents with
this broad and dynamic "memory" as a crucial building block to actualize this vision, and we argue that current RAG
approaches are unsuitable for this future. Since entire conversation histories, business datasets, and other domainspecific content cannot fit effectively inside LLM context windows, new approaches need to be developed for agent
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
memory. Adding memory to LLM-powered agents isn’t a new idea—this concept has been explored previously in
MemGPT [3].
Recently, Knowledge Graphs (KGs) have been employed to enhance RAG architectures to address many of the shortcomings of traditional IR techniques[4]. In this paper, we introduce Zep[5], a memory layer service powered by
Graphiti[6], a dynamic, temporally-aware knowledge graph engine. Zep ingests and synthesizes both unstructured
message data and structured business data. The Graphiti KG engine dynamically updates the knowledge graph with
new information in n-lossy manner, maintaining a timeline of facts and relationships, including their periods of
validity. This approach enables the knowledge graph to represent a complex, evolving world.
As Zep is a production system, we’ve focused heavily on the accuracy, latency, and scalability of its memory retrieval
mechanisms. We evaluate these mechanisms’ efficacy using two existing benchmarks: a Deep Memory Retrieval task
(DMR) from MemGPT[3], as well as the LongMemEval benchmark[7].
2 Knowledge Graph Construction
In Zep, memory is powered by a temporally-aware dynamic knowledge graph G = (N , E, φ), where N represents
nodes, E represents edges, and φ : E → N × N represents a formal incidence function. This graph comprises three
hierarchical tiers of subgraphs: an episode subgraph, a semantic entity subgraph, and a community subgraph.
• Episode Subgraph Ge: Episodic nodes (episodes), ni ∈ Ne, contain raw input data in the form of messages, text, or JSON. Episodes serve as a non-lossy data store from whictities and relations are
extracted. Episodic edges, ei ∈ Ee ⊆ φ
∗
(Ne × Ns), connect episodes to their referenced semantic entities.
• Semantic Entity Subgraph Gs: The semantic entity subgraph builds upon the episode subgraph. Entity
nodes (entities), ni ∈ Ns, represent entities extracted from episodes and resolved with existing graph entities.
Entity edges (semantic edges), ei ∈ Es ⊆ φ
∗
(Ns × Ns), represent relationships between entities extracted
from episodes.
• Community Subgraph Gc: The community subgraph forms the highest level of Zep’s knowledge graph.
Community nodes (communities), ni ∈ Nc, represent clusters of strongly connected entities. Communities
contain high-level summarizations of these clusters and represent a more comprehensive, interconnected view
of Gs’s structure. Community edges, ei ∈ Ec ⊆ φ
∗
(Nc × Ns), connect communities to their entity members.
The dual storage of both raw episodic data and derived semantic entity information mirrors psychologicels distinguish between episodic memory, which represents distinct events, and semantic
memory, which captures associations between concepts and their meanings [8]. This approach enables LLM agents
using Zep to develop more sophisticated and nuanced memory structures that better align with our understanding of
human memory systems. Knowledge graphs provide an effective medium for representing these memory structures,
and our implementation of distinct episodic and semantic subgraphs draws from similar approaches in AriGraph [9].
Our use of community nodes to represent high-level structures and domain concepts builds upon work from GraphRAG
[4], enabling a more comprehensive global understanding of the domain. The resulting hierarchical organization—from episodes to facts to entities to communities—extends existing hierarchical RAG strategies [10][11].
2.1 Episodes
Zep’s graph construction begins with the ingestion of raw data units called Episodes. Episodes can be one of three
core types: message, texJSON. While each type requires specific handling during graph construction, this paper
focuses on the message type, as our experiments center on conversation memory. In our context, a message consists
of relatively short text (several messages can fit within an LLM context window) along with the associated actor who
produced the utterance.
Each message includes a reference timestamp tref indicating when the message was sent. This temporal information
enables Zep to accurately identify and extract relative or partial dates mentioned in the message content (e.g., "next
Thursday," "in two weeks," or "last summer"). Zep implements a bi-temporal model, where timeline T represents the
chronological ordering of events, and timeline T
′
represents the transactional order of Zep’s data ingestion. While
the T
′
timeline serves the traditional purpose of database auditing, the T timeline provides an additional dimension
for modeling the dynamic nature of conversational data and memory. This bi-temporal approach ents a novel
advancement in LLM-based knowledge graph construction and underlies much of Zep’s unique capabilities compared
to previous graph-based RAG proposals.
2
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
The episodic edges, Ee, connect episodes to their extracted entity nodes. Episodes and their derived semantic edges
maintain bidirectional indices that track the relationships between edges and their source episodes. This design reinforces the non-lossy nature of Graphiti’s episodic subgraph by enabling both forward and backward traversal: semantic
artifacts can be traced to their sources for citation or quotation, while episodes can quickly retrieve their relevant entities and facts. While these connections are not directly examined in this paper’s experiments, they will be explored in
future work.
2.2 Semantic Entities and Facts
2.2.1 Entities
ntity extraction represents the initial phase of episode processing. During ingestion, the system processes both the
current message content and then messages to provide context for named entity recognition. For this paper and
in Zep’s general implementation, n = 4, providing two complete conversation turns for context evaluation. Given our
focus on message processing, the speaker is automatically extracted as an entity. Following initial entity extraction, we
employ a reflection technique inspired by reflexion[12] to minimize hallucinations and enhance extraction coverage.
The system also extracts an entity summary from the episode to facilitate subsequent entity resolution and retrieval
operations.
After extraction, the system embeds each entity name into a 1024-dimensional vector space. This embedding enables
the retrieval of similar nodes through cosine similarity search across existing graph entity nodes. The system also performs a separate full-text search on existing entity names and summaries to identify additional candidate nodes. These
candidate nodes, together with the episode context, are then processed through an LLM using our entity resution
prompt. When the system identifies a duplicate entity, it generates an updated name and summary.
Following entity extraction and resolution, the system incorporates the data into the knowledge graph using predefined
Cypher queries. We chose this approach over LLM-generated database queries to ensure consistent schema formats
and reduce the potential for hallucinations.
Selected prompts for graph construction are provided in the appendix.
2.2.2 Facts
or each fact containing its key predicate. Importantly, the same fact can be extracted multiple times between different
entities, enabling Graphiti to model complex multi-entity facts through an implementation of hyper-edges.
Following extraction, the system generates embeddings for facts in preparation for graph integration. The system
performs edge deduplication through a process similar to entity resolution. The hybrid search for relevant edges is
constrained to edges existing between the same entity pairs as the proposed new edge. This constraint not only prevents
erroneous combinations of similar edges between different entities but also significantly reduces the computational
complexity of the deduplication process by limiting the search space to a subset of edges relevant to the specific entity
pair.
2.2.3 Temporal Extraction and Edge Invalidation
A key differentiating feature of Graphiti compared to other knowledge graph engines is its capacity to manage dynamic
information updates through temporal extraction and edge invalidation processes.
The system extracts temporal information about facts from the episode context using tref. This enables accurate extraction and datetime representation of both absolute timestamps (e.g., "Alan Turing was born on June 23, 1912") and
relative timestamps (e.g., "I started my new job two weeks ago"). Consistent with our bi-temporal modeling approach,
the system tracks four timestamps: t
′
created and t
′
expired ∈ T
′ monitor when facts are created or invalidated in the
system, while tvalid and tinvalid ∈ T temporal range during which facts held true. These temporal data points
are stored on edges alongside other fact information.
The introduction of new edges can invalidate existing edges in the database. The system employs an LLM to compare
new edges against semantically related existing edges to identify potential contradictions. When the system identifies temporally overlapping contradictions, it invalidates the affected edges by setting their tinvalid to the tvalid of the
invalidating edge. Following the transactional timeline T
′
, Graphiti consistently prioritizes new information when
determining edge invalidation.
This comprehensive approach enables the dynamic addition of data to Graphiti as conversations evolve, while maintaining both current relationship states and historical records of relationship evolution over time.
3
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
2.3 Communities
After establishing the episodic and semantic subgraphs, the system constructs the community subgraph through commity detection. While our community detection approach builds upon the technique described in GraphRAG[4],
we employ a label propagation algorithm [13] rather than the Leiden algorithm [14]. This choice was influenced by
label propagation’s straightforward dynamic extension, which enables the system to maintain accurate community
representations for longer periods as new data enters the graph, delaying the need for complete community refreshes.
The dynamic extension implements the logic of a single recursive step in label propagation. When the system adds
a new entity node ni ∈ Ns to the graph, it surveys the communities of neighboring nodes. The system assigns
the new node to the community held by the plurality of its neighbors, then updates the community summary and
graph accordingly. While this dynamic updating enables efficient community extension as data flows into the system,
the resulting communities gradually diverge from those that would be generated by a complete label propagation
run. Thereforeriodic community refreshes remain necessary. However, this dynamic updating strategy provides a
practical heuristic that significantly reduces latency and LLM inference costs.
Following [4], our community nodes contain summaries derived through an iterative map-reduce-style summarization
of member nodes. However, our retrieval methods differ substantially from GraphRAG’s map-reduce approach [4].
To support our retrieval methodology, we generate community names containing key terms and relevant subjects from
the community summaries. These names are embedded and stored to enable cosine similarity searches.
3 Memory Retrieval
The memory retrieval system in Zep provides powerful, complex, and highly configurable functionality. At a high
level, the Zep graph search API implements a function f : S → S that accepts a text-string query α ∈ S as input and
returns a text-string context β ∈ S as output. The output β contains formatted data from nodes and edges required for
an LLM agent to generate an accure to query α. The process f(α) → β comprises three distinct steps:
• Search (ϕ): The process begins by identifying candidate nodes and edges potentially containing relevant
information. While Zep employs multiple distinct search methods, the overall search function can be represented as ϕ : S → En

• Reranker (ρ): The second step reorders search results. A reranker function or model accepts a list of search
results and produces a reordered version of those results: ρ : ϕ(α), ... → En

3.1 Search
Zep implements three search functions: cosine semantic similarity search (ϕcos), Okapi BM25 full-text search (ϕbm25),
and breadth-first search (ϕbfs). The first two functions utilize Neo4j’s implementation of Lucene [15][16]. Each
4
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
search function offers distinct capabilities in identifying relevant documents, and together they provide comprehensive
coverage of candidate results before reranking. The search field varies across the three object typ we search
the fact field; for Ns, the entity name; and for Nc, the community name, which comprises relevant keywords and
phrases covered in the community. While developed independently, our community search approach parallels the
high-level key search methodology in LightRAG [17]. The hybridization of LightRAG’s approach with graph-based
systems like Graphiti presents a promising direction for future research.
While cosine similarity and full-text search methodologies are well-established in RAG [18], breadth-first search over
knowledge graphs has received limited attention in the RAG domain, with notable exceptions in graph-based RAG
systems such as AriGraph [9] and Distill-SynthKG [19]. In Graphiti, the breadth-first search enhances initial search
results by identifying additional nodes and edges within n-hops. Moreover, ϕbfs can accept nodes as parameters for the
search, enabling greater control over the search function. This functionality proves particularly valuable when using
recent episodes as se for the breadth-first search, allowing the system to incorporate recently mentioned entities
and relationships into the retrieved context.
The three search methods each target different aspects of similarity: full-text search identifies word similarities, cosine
similarity captures semantic similarities, and breadth-first search reveals contextual similarities—where nodes and
edges closer in the graph appear in more similar conversational contexts. This multi-faceted approach to candidate
result identification maximizes the likelihood of discovering optimal context.
3.2 Reranker
While the initial search methods aim to achieve high recall, rerankers serve to increase precision by prioritizing the
most relevant results. Zep supports existing reranking approaches such as Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) [20] and
Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) [21]. Additionally, Zep implements a graph-based episode-mentions reranker
that prioritizes results based on the frequency of entity or fact mentions within a conversati, enabling a system
where frequently referenced information becomes more readily accessible. The system also includes a node distance
reranker that reorders results based on their graph distance from a designated centroid node, providing context localized to specific areas of the knowledge graph. The system’s most sophisticated reranking capability employs crossencoders—LLMs that generate relevance scores by evaluating nodes and edges against queries using cross-attention,
though this approach incurs the highest computational cost.
4 Experiments
This section analyzes two experiments conducted using LLM-memory based benchmarks. The first evaluation employs
the Deep Memory Retrieval (DMR) task developed in [3], which uses a 500-conversation subset of the Multi-Session
Chat dataset introduced in "Beyond Goldfish Memory: Long-Term Open-Domain Conversation" [22]. The second
evaluation utilizes the LongMemEval benchmark from "LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term
Interactive Memory" [7]. Specally, we use the LongMemEvals dataset, which provides an extensive conversation
context of on average 115,000 tokens.
For both experiments, we integrate the conversation history into a Zep knowledge graph through Zep’s APIs. We
then retrieve the 20 most relevant edges (facts) and entity nodes (entity summaries) using the techniques described in
Section 3. The system reformats this data into a context string, matching the functionality provided by Zep’s memory
APIs.
While these experiments demonstrate key retrieval capabilities of Graphiti, they represent a subset of the system’s
full search functionality. This focused scope enables clear comparison with existing benchmarks while reserving the
exploration of additional knowledge graph capabilities for future work.
4.1 Choice of models
Our experimental implementation employs the BGE-m3 models from BAAI for both reranking and embedding tasks
[23] [24]. For graph construction and response generation, we utilize gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 for graph constructio both gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 and gpt-4o-2024-11-20 for the chat agent generating responses to provided context.
To ensure direct comparability with MemGPT’s DMR results, we also conducted the DMR evaluation using gpt-4-
turbo-2024-04-09.
The experimental notebooks will be made publicly available through our GitHub repository, and relevant experimental
prompts are included in the Appendix.

4.2 Deep Memory Retrieval (DMR)
The Deep Memory Retrieval evaluation, introduced by [3], comprises 500 multi-session conversations, each containing
5 chat sessions with up to 12 messages per session. Each conversation includes on/answer pair for memory
evaluation. The MemGPT framework [3] currently leads performance metrics with 93.4% accuracy using gpt-4-turbo,
a significant improvement over the 35.3% baseline achieved through recursive summarization.
To establish comparative baselines, we implemented two common LLM memory approaches: full-conversation context and session summaries. Using gpt-4-turbo, the full-conversation baseline achieved 94.4% accuracy, slightly surpassing MemGPT’s reported results, while the session summary baseline achieved 78.6%. When using gpt-4o-mini,
both approaches showed improved performance: 98.0% for full-conversation and 88.0% for session summaries. We
were unable to reproduce MemGPT’s results using gpt-4o-mini due to insufficient methodological details in their
published work.
We then evaluated Zep’s performance by ingesting the conversations and using its search functions to retrieve the top
10 most relevant nodes and edges. An LLM judge compared the agent’s responses to the provided goldrs.
Zep achieved 94.8% accuracy with gpt-4-turbo and 98.2% with gpt-4o-mini, showing marginal improvements over
both MemGPT and the respective full-conversation baselines. However, these results must be contextualized: each
conversation contains only 60 messages, easily fitting within current LLM context windows.
The limitations of the DMR evaluation extend beyond its small scale. Our analysis revealed significant weaknesses
in the benchmark’s design. The evaluation relies exclusively on single-turn, fact-retrieval questions that fail to assess
complex memory understanding. Many questions contain ambiguous phrasing, referencing concepts like "favorite
drink to relax with" or "weird hobby" that were not explicitly characterized as such in the conversations. Most critically,
the dataset poorly represents real-world enterprise use cases for LLM agents. The high performance achieved by
simple full-context approaches using modern LLMs further highlights the benchmark’s inadequacy for evaluating
memory systemhis inadequacy is further emphasized by findings in [7], which demonstrate rapidly declining LLM performance on
the LongMemEval benchmark as conversation length increases. The LongMemEval dataset [7] addresses many of
these shortcomings by presenting longer, more coherent conversations that better reflect enterprise scenarios, along
with more diverse evaluation questions.
4.3 LongMemEval (LME)
We evaluated Zep using the LongMemEvals dataset, which provides conversations and questions representative of realworld business applications of LLM agents. The LongMemEvals dataset presents significant challenges to existing
LLMs and commercial memory solutions [7], with conversations averaging approximately 115,000 tokens in length.
This length, while substantial, remains within the context windows of current frontier models, enabling us to establish
meaningful baselines for evaluating Zep’s performance.
The dataset incorporates six distinct question types: single-session-user, single-session-assistant, single-sesonpreference, multi-session, knowledge-update, and temporal-reasoning. These categories are not uniformly distributed
throughout the dataset; for detailed distribution information, we refer readers to [7].
We conducted all experiments between December 2024 and January 2025. We performed testing using a consumer
laptop from a residential location in Boston, MA, connecting to Zep’s service hosted in AWS us-west-2. This dis6
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
tributed architecture introduced additional network latency when evaluating Zep’s performance, though this latency
was not present in our baseline evaluations.
For answer evaluation, we employed GPT-4o with the question-specific prompts provided in [7], which have demonstrated high correlation with human evaluators.
4.3.1 LongMemEval and MemGPT
To establish a comparative benchmark between Zep and the current state-of-the-art MemGPT system [3], we attempted
to evaluate MemGPT using the LongMemEval dataset. Given that the current MemGPT frork does not support
direct ingestion of existing message histories, we implemented a workaround by adding conversation messages to the
archival history. However, we were unable to achieve successful question responses using this approach. We look
forward to seeing evaluations of this benchmark by other research teams, as comparative performance data would
benefit the broader development of LLM memory systems.
4.3.2 LongMemEval results
Zep demonstrates substantial improvements in both accuracy and latency compared to the baseline across both model
variants. Using gpt-4o-mini, Zep achieved a 15.2% accuracy improvement over the baseline, while gpt-4o showed an
18.5% improvement. The reduced prompt size also led to significant latency cost reductions compared to the baseline
implementations.

The decrease in performance for single-session-assistant questions—17.7% for gpt-4o and 9.06% for gpt-4omini—represents a notable exception to Zep’s otherwise consistent improvements, and suggest further research and
engineering work is needed.
5 Conclusion
We have introduced Zep, a graph-based approach to LLM memory that incorporates semantic and episodic memory
alongside entity and community summaries. Our evate that Zep achieves state-of-the-art performance on existing memory benchmarks while reducing token costs and operating at significantly lower latencies.
The results achieved with Graphiti and Zep, while impressive, likely represent only initial advances in graph-based
memory systems. Multiple research avenues could build upon these frameworks, including integration of other
GraphRAG approaches into the Zep paradigm and novel extensions of our work.
Research has already demonstrated the value of fine-tuned models for LLM-based entity and edge extraction within
the GraphRAG paradigm, improving accuracy while reducing costs and latency [19][25]. Similar models finetuned for Graphiti prompts may enhance knowledge extraction, particularly for complex conversations. Additionally, while current research on LLM-generated knowledge graphs has primarily operated without formal ontologies
[9][4][17][19][26], domain-specific ontologies present significant potential. Graph ontologies, foundational in preLLM knowledge graph work, warrant further exploration within the Graphiti framework.
Our search for suitable memory benchmarks revealed limited options, with existing benchmarks often lacking robustness and complexity, frequently defaulting to simple needle-in-a-haystack fact-retrieval questions [3]. The field
requires additional memory benchmarks, particularly those reflecting business applications like customer experience
tasks, to effectively evaluate and differentiate memory approaches. Notably, no existing benchmarks adequately assess
Zep’s capability to process and synthesize conversation history with structured business data. While Zep focuses on
LLM memory, its traditional RAG capabilities should be evaluated against established benchmarks such as those in
[17], [27], and [28].
Current literature on LLM memory and RAG systems insufficiently addresses production system scalability in terms
of cost and latency. We have included latency benchmarks for our retrieval mechanisms to begin addressing this gap,
followinthe example set by LightRAG’s authors in prioritizing these metrics.

1. Extract facts only between the provided entities.
2. Each fact should represent a clear relationship between two DISTINCT nodes.
3. The relation_type should be a concise, all-caps description of the fact (e.g., LOVES, IS_FRIENDS_WITH,
WORKS_FOR).
4. Provide a more detailed fact containing all relevant information.
5. Consider temporal aspects of relationships when relevant.
9
Using Knowledge Graphs to power LLM-Agent Memory
6.1.4 Fact Resolution
Given the following context, determine whether the New Edge represents any of the edges in the list of Existing Edges.
<EXISTING EDGES>
{existing_edges}
</EXISTING EDGES>
<NEW EDGE>
{new_edge}
</NEW EDGE>
Task:
1. If the New Edges represents the same factual information as any edge in Existing Edges, return ’is_duplicate: true’
in the response. Otherwise, return ’is_duplicate: false’
2. If is_duplicate is true, also return the uuid of the existing edge in the response
Guidelines:
1. The  not need to be completely identical to be duplicates, they just need to express the same information.
6.1.5 Temporal Extraction
<PREVIOUS MESSAGES>
{previous_messages}
</PREVIOUS MESSAGES>
<CURRENT MESSAGE>
{current_message}
</CURRENT MESSAGE>
<REFERENCE TIMESTAMP>
{reference_timestamp}
</REFERENCE TIMESTAMP>
<FACT>
{fact}
</FACT>
IMPORTANT: Only extract time information if it is part of the provided fact. Otherwise ignore the time mentioned.
Make sure to do your best to determine the dates if only the relative time is mentioned. (eg 10 years ago, 2 mins ago)
based on the provided reference timestamp
If the relationship is not of spanning nature, but you are still able to determine the dates, set the valid_at only.
Definitions:
- valid_at: The date and time when the relationship described by the edge fact became true or was established.
- invalid_at: The date and time when the relationship described by the edge fact stopped being true or ended.
Task:
Analyze the conversation and determine if there are dates that are part of the edge fact. Only set dates if they explicitly
relate to the formation or alteration of the relationship itself.
Guidelines:
1. Use ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.SSSSSSZ) for datetimes.
2. Use the reference timestamp as the current time when determining the valid_at and invalid_at dates.
3. If the fact is written in the present tense, use the Reference Timestamp for the valid_at date
4. If no temporal information is found that establishes or changes the relationship, leave the fields as null.
5. Do not infer dates from related events. Only use dates that are directly stated to establish or change the relationship.
6. For relative time mentions directly related to the relationship, calculate the actual datetime based on the reference
timestamp.
7. If only a date is mentioned without a specific time, use 00:00:00 (midnight) for that date.
8. If only year is mentioned, use January 1st of that year at 00:00:00.
9. Always include the time zone offset (use Z for UTC if no specific time zone is mentioned).

 `./server/PLAN.md`

```text
1	# Zep-style Memory System — Implementation Plan
2	
3	A practical implementation of the Zep paper: a temporal knowledge-graph backed memory layer for LLM agents. Episodes (raw messages) are captured immutably, then a background pipeline distills them into entities, time-stamped facts, and community summaries that an agent can query through hybrid (vector + graph) retrieval.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Stack
8	
9	| Layer | Choice | Purpose |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| API | **Bun + Hono** (port 3002) | Thin, fast HTTP layer |
12	| Relational | **Neon Postgres** + Drizzle ORM | Durable raw episode store, auth, audit trail |
13	| Vector | **Pinecone** | Semantic recall over entities + facts |
14	| Graph | **Neo4j** | Temporal knowledge graph (3 subgraphs) |
15	| Queue | **BullMQ** on **Redis** (`ioredis`) | Background extraction pipeline |
16	| LLM | **OpenAI** (`openai` package) | Entity + fact + temporal extraction, optional reranker |
17	| Validation | **Zod** | Request/response schemas |
18	| Auth | Email + password (basic, no OAuth) | Tenancy boundary |
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 2. The two-path architecture
23	
24	The system splits work into a fast write path and a slower read path. The user-facing API never blocks on LLM calls.
25	
26	```
27	                       ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
28	                       │           USER / CLIENT          │
29	                       └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
30	                                      │
31	                ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
32	                │ POST /ingest                              │ POST /retrieve
33	                ▼                                           ▼
34	        ┌───────────────┐                          ┌────────────────┐
35	        │  Hono route   │                          │  Hono route    │
36	        └───────┬───────┘                          └────────┬───────┘
37	                │ 1. insert episode (Postgres)              │
38	                │ 2. enqueue BullMQ job                     │
39	                │ 3. return 202 { episodeId }               │
40	                ▼                                           │
41	        ┌───────────────────────┐                           ▼
42	        │ BullMQ worker         │                  ┌────────────────┐
43	        │ (memory-ingestion)    │                  │ memoryRetrieval │
44	        │                       │                  │   .getContext()  │
45	        │  LLM extract entities │                  │                │
46	        │  → resolve entities   │                  │  Pinecone      │
47	        │  → LLM extract facts  │                  │  → Neo4j hops  │
48	        │  → temporal stamps    │                  │  → temporal    │
49	        │  → write Neo4j        │                  │    filter      │
50	        │  → upsert Pinecone    │                  │  → rerank      │
51	        │  → update status PG   │                  │  → build text  │
52	        └───────────────────────┘                  └────────────────┘
53	```
54	
55	---
56	
57	## 3. Storage layers — what lives where
58	
59	| Store | Holds | Why this and not another |
60	|---|---|---|
61	| **Postgres** | `users`, `sessions`, `episodes` (raw), `processing_logs` | Durability + relational queries. If everything else burns down, no message is lost. |
62	| **Neo4j** | `:Episode`, `:Entity`, `:Community` nodes; `:MENTIONS`, `:FACT`, `:CONTAINS` edges | Multi-hop traversal + temporal filtering. SQL would need recursive CTEs and is awkward for this. |
63	| **Pinecone** | One vector per entity, one per fact. Metadata: `type`, `userId`, `neo4jId` | Semantic match. Graph alone misses paraphrases. |
64	| **Redis (BullMQ)** | Job queues, retries, optional retrieval cache | Decouples API latency from LLM latency. |
65	
66	Mental model: **Postgres is durability, Neo4j is structure-and-time, Pinecone is similarity, BullMQ is patience.**
67	
68	---
69	
70	## 4. Neo4j — the three-subgraph design
71	
72	Not three databases. **One graph, three logical layers** distinguished by node label.
73	
74	### Layer 1 — Episode subgraph (raw)
75	```cypher
76	CREATE (e:Episode {
77	  episodeId, content, actor, timestamp, userId, sessionId, createdAt
78	})
79	MATCH (e:Episode {episodeId: $episodeId})
80	MATCH (ent:Entity {entityId: $entityId})
81	CREATE (e)-[:MENTIONS { role: "source", timestamp: $timestamp }]->(ent)
82	```
83	Bottom layer. One `:Episode` per raw message. Connects to entities via `:MENTIONS`.
84	
85	### Layer 2 — Semantic Entity subgraph (the meat)
86	```cypher
87	MERGE (ent:Entity { entityId: $entityId })
88	SET ent.name = $name,
89	    ent.type = $type,           -- PERSON, COMPANY, CONCEPT, ...
90	    ent.summary = $summary,
91	    ent.userId = $userId,
92	    ent.updatedAt = datetime()
93	
94	MATCH (a:Entity { entityId: $sourceId })
95	MATCH (b:Entity { entityId: $targetId })
96	MERGE (a)-[f:FACT {
97	  factId: $factId,
98	  relationType: $relationType,  -- WORKS_AT, LIKES, FRIENDS_WITH, ...
99	  factText: $factText,
100	  validFrom: $validFrom,
101	  validUntil: $validUntil,      -- null/∞ until contradicted
102	  confidence: $confidence,
103	  episodeId: $episodeId
104	}]->(b)
105	```
106	Middle layer. `:Entity` nodes deduplicated by entity-resolver. `:FACT` edges carry **bi-temporal** properties (`validFrom`/`validUntil`) — the property that makes the system "Zep-like."
107	
108	#### Temporal invalidation (the Graphiti pattern)
109	
110	When the worker extracts a new fact that contradicts an existing open-ended fact (same `sourceEntity` + `relationType`, `validUntil IS NULL`), it does **not delete** the old edge. It closes the old edge by setting `validUntil = newFact.validFrom`, then writes the new edge. This preserves full history and lets retrieval answer "what did the user believe in March?" vs "what's true now?".
111	
112	```cypher
113	// 1. close out the conflicting open-ended edge (if any)
114	MATCH (a:Entity { entityId: $sourceId })-[old:FACT { relationType: $relationType }]->(:Entity)
115	WHERE old.validUntil IS NULL
116	SET old.validUntil = $newValidFrom
117	
118	// 2. write the new edge
119	MATCH (a:Entity { entityId: $sourceId })
120	MATCH (b:Entity { entityId: $newTargetId })
121	MERGE (a)-[:FACT {
122	  factId: $factId,
123	  relationType: $relationType,
124	  factText: $newFactText,
125	  validFrom: $newValidFrom,
126	  validUntil: null,
127	  confidence: $confidence,
128	  episodeId: $episodeId
129	}]->(b)
130	```
131	
132	Edge cases the worker must handle:
133	- **Re-affirmation** (`"I still like dogs"`) — don't create a duplicate; bump `confidence` or skip.
134	- **Predicate ambiguity** (`loves` vs `likes`) — rules alone will miss; needs an LLM disambiguation step.
135	- **Partial contradiction** (`"I love dogs but cats more"`) — keep both edges with overlapping validity instead of invalidating.
136	- **Different target** (`"liked dogs" → "likes cats"`) — scope conflict detection by `source + relationType`, **not** `source + target`, otherwise the old edge stays open forever.
137	
138	Pinecone fact vectors mirror `validFrom`/`validUntil` so coarse temporal pruning happens at the vector layer too. Retrieval filters `coalesce(validUntil, '9999-01-01') >= now AND validFrom <= now` for "current" facts; drop the filter to walk history.
139	
140	### Layer 3 — Community subgraph (summaries)
141	```cypher
142	MERGE (c:Community { communityId: $communityId })
143	SET c.name = $name,
144	    c.summary = $summary,
145	    c.userId = $userId,
146	    c.updatedAt = datetime()
147	
148	MATCH (c:Community { communityId: $communityId })
149	MATCH (e:Entity { entityId: $entityId })
150	MERGE (c)-[:CONTAINS]->(e)
151	```
152	Top layer. Built periodically by a low-priority queue. Used to inject "context about who/what this conversation is broadly about" into retrieval.
153	
154	### Connection picture
155	```
156	Episode  ──MENTIONS──▶  Entity  ──FACT──▶  Entity
157	                          ▲
158	                          │ CONTAINS
159	                       Community
160	```
161	
162	---
163	
164	## 5. Postgres schema
165	
166	All tables scope by `userId` for tenancy.
167	
168	| Table | Columns | Role |
169	|---|---|---|
170	| `users` | `id`, `email` (unique), `password_hash`, `created_at` | Auth |
171	| `sessions` | `id`, `user_id` (FK→users), `title`, `created_at` | Conversation grouping |
172	| `episodes` | `id`, `user_id`, `session_id`, `actor` (enum), `content`, `status` (enum), `metadata` (jsonb), `occurred_at`, `created_at`, `processed_at` | Raw immutable log + worker status |
173	| `processing_logs` | `id`, `episode_id` (FK), `step`, `status`, `message`, `duration_ms`, `created_at` | Per-step pipeline audit trail |
174	
175	Enums:
176	- `actor`: `user | assistant | system`
177	- `episode_status`: `queued | processing | processed | failed`
178	
179	Indexes:
180	- `episodes (user_id, session_id, occurred_at)` — for "last N messages of session"
181	- `episodes (status)` — for worker retry/inspection
182	- `sessions (user_id)`
183	- `processing_logs (episode_id)`
184	
185	---
186	
187	## 6. Pinecone index design
188	
189	- **One index** (single embedder dimension), distinguished by metadata.
190	- Required metadata on every vector: `userId`, `type` (`"entity" | "fact"`), `neo4jId`, `episodeId`.
191	- Optional: `validFrom`, `validUntil` mirrored on fact vectors so coarse temporal pruning happens at the vector layer too.
192	- Query filter is always `{ userId: $uid, ... }` — never run an unfiltered query.
193	
194	---
195	
196	## 7. Auth
197	
198	Basic — focus on the core workflow, not auth ceremony.
199	
200	- `POST /auth/signup` → email + password → bcrypt hash → row in `users` → return JWT
201	- `POST /auth/login` → verify hash → return JWT
202	- All ingest/retrieve routes require a Bearer JWT; `userId` is derived from the token, never trusted from the body.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## 8. Ingestion pipeline (worker)
207	
208	`POST /ingest { sessionId, actor, content, occurredAt? }`:
209	
210	1. Hono route inserts an `episodes` row (status=`queued`, `occurred_at` = client-provided or server-now).
211	2. `ingestionQueue.add("process-episode", { episodeId })` with `attempts: 3`, exponential backoff.
212	3. Return `202 { episodeId, status: "queued" }`.
213	
214	Worker (separate Bun process) picks up the job:
215	
216	| Step | Action | Writes |
217	|---|---|---|
218	| 1 | Mark episode `processing` | Postgres |
219	| 2 | Fetch episode + last 6 messages of session | Postgres read |
220	| 3 | LLM call: extract raw entities | — |
221	| 4 | Entity resolver: dedupe against existing `:Entity` nodes scoped to `userId` | Neo4j read |
222	| 5 | LLM call: extract facts between resolved entities | — |
223	| 6a | **Contradiction detection**: for each new fact, find existing open-ended edges (`validUntil IS NULL`) scoped to the same `sourceEntity + relationType`. Optional LLM disambiguation step for ambiguous predicates. | Neo4j read |
224	| 6b | **Temporal annotation + invalidation**: assign `validFrom = episode.occurredAt`; for every contradicting old edge, set `validUntil = newFact.validFrom`. Old edges are **closed, never deleted**. | — |
225	| 7 | `Promise.all`: write Neo4j (new edges + closed-out edges), upsert Pinecone (mirror `validFrom`/`validUntil` on fact vectors), mark episode `processed` | Neo4j + Pinecone + Postgres |
226	| 8 | If threshold hit, enqueue `community-update` for this user | Redis |
227	
228	Each step writes a row to `processing_logs` with `duration_ms`.
229	
230	Failures retry per BullMQ; final failure marks episode `failed` and lands in a DLQ.
231	
232	---
233	
234	## 9. Retrieval pipeline
235	
236	`POST /retrieve { sessionId?, query, limit? }`:
237	
238	1. Embed query (OpenAI).
239	2. `pinecone.query({ filter: { userId } }, topK: 20–30)` — returns entity + fact vectors.
240	3. From those `neo4jId`s, run a 1–2 hop Neo4j traversal **with temporal filter** `WHERE coalesce(f.validUntil, datetime('9999-01-01')) >= now AND f.validFrom <= now`.
241	4. Optionally pull the user's most relevant `:Community` summaries via `[:CONTAINS]`.
242	5. Rerank: weighted blend of `vector_score`, `graph_proximity`, `recency`. Optional LLM reranker on top-K.
243	6. Build context: facts with their date ranges + last 3–5 raw messages from Postgres for grounding.
244	7. Return ~1–3k tokens.
245	
246	---
247	
248	## 10. API surface (initial)
249	
250	| Method | Path | Purpose |
251	|---|---|---|
252	| POST | `/auth/signup` | Create account |
253	| POST | `/auth/login` | Get JWT |
254	| POST | `/sessions` | Create a session |
255	| GET  | `/sessions` | List user's sessions |
256	| POST | `/ingest` | Submit a message (returns 202) |
257	| GET  | `/episodes/:id` | Inspect an episode + its processing log |
258	| POST | `/retrieve` | Get hybrid context for a query |
259	
260	---
261	
262	## 11. Phased build order
263	
264	1. **Schema + migrations** — `users`, `sessions`, `episodes`, `processing_logs`. ✅ in progress
265	2. **Auth** — signup, login, JWT middleware.
266	3. **Session + ingest routes** (return 202; no worker yet — episode just sits in `queued`).
267	4. **BullMQ + Redis** — queue setup + worker harness, no LLM yet.
268	5. **Neo4j service** — driver, episode/entity/fact write helpers, indices on `entityId`/`userId`.
269	6. **OpenAI extraction** — entity prompt, fact prompt, temporal prompt; structured output via Zod.
270	7. **Entity resolver** — dedupe pass against Neo4j scoped to user.
271	8. **Pinecone service** — embed + upsert with metadata.
272	9. **Wire the worker** — full pipeline end to end on a single test episode.
273	10. **Retrieval service** — Pinecone → Neo4j expansion → temporal filter → rerank → context build.
274	11. **Communities** — periodic clustering + summary, low-priority queue.
275	12. **Observability** — BullMQ UI, processing-log dashboard, simple metrics.
276	
277	---
278	
279	## 12. Open decisions to revisit
280	
281	- **Bi-temporal vs uni-temporal.** Currently planning event-time only (`validFrom`/`validUntil`). Zep's paper also tracks transaction time (when system *learned* a fact). Adding it later means a graph migration — decide before step 5.
282	- **Worker process model.** Same Bun process or `bun run workers/index.ts` separately. Lean toward separate for restart isolation.
283	- **Community cadence.** Every N episodes per user vs debounced timer. Lean toward N=20 with a per-user lock.
284	- **Pinecone single-index vs namespaces.** Going single-index + metadata filter. Revisit if cross-user volume becomes asymmetric.
285	- **JWT vs session cookies.** Going JWT for simplicity since there's no browser session story yet.
286	- **Contradiction detection — rules or LLM?** Default to rule-based (`source + relationType + validUntil IS NULL`). Add an LLM disambiguation pass for ambiguous predicates (`loves` vs `likes`) and partial contradictions. Revisit if precision is poor.
287
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	import { Worker, type Job } from "bullmq";
2	import { eq } from "drizzle-orm";
3	import { db } from "../models/db";
4	import { episodes, processingLogs } from "../models/schema";
5	import {
6	  INGESTION_QUEUE_NAME,
7	  type IngestionJobData,
8	} from "../queues/ingestion.queue";
9	import { redisConnection } from "../queues/connection";
10	import {
11	  fetchEpisodeById,
12	  getRecentMessages,
13	} from "../services/episodes.service";
14	import {
15	  extractEntities,
16	  extractFacts,
17	} from "../services/extraction.service";
18	import {
19	  resolveEntities,
20	  getOrCreateSelfEntity,
21	} from "../services/resolver.service";
22	import { applyTemporalFacts } from "../services/temporal.service";
23	import {
24	  upsertEntityVectors,
25	  upsertFactVectors,
26	} from "../services/pinecone.service";
27	import {
28	  createEpisodeNode,
29	  linkEpisodeToEntities,
30	} from "../services/neo4j.service";
31	
32	async function logStep(
33	  episodeId: string,
34	  stepName: string,
35	  status: "ok" | "error",
36	  durationMs: number,
37	  message?: string,
38	) {
39	  await db.insert(processingLogs).values({
40	    episodeId,
41	    step: stepName,
42	    status,
43	    durationMs,
44	    message: message ?? null,
45	  });
46	}
47	
48	async function step<T>(
49	  episodeId: string,
50	  stepName: string,
51	  fn: () => Promise<T>,
52	): Promise<T> {
53	  const t0 = Date.now();
54	  try {
55	    const result = await fn();
56	    await logStep(episodeId, stepName, "ok", Date.now() - t0);
57	    return result;
58	  } catch (err) {
59	    const msg = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
60	    await logStep(episodeId, stepName, "error", Date.now() - t0, msg);
61	    throw err;
62	  }
63	}
64	
65	function tag(episodeId: string): string {
66	  return `[${episodeId.slice(0, 8)}]`;
67	}
68	
69	function nameById(
70	  list: Array<{ entityId: string; name: string }>,
71	  id: string,
72	): string {
73	  return list.find((e) => e.entityId === id)?.name ?? "?";
74	}
75	
76	async function processEpisode(job: Job<IngestionJobData>) {
77	  const { episodeId } = job.data;
78	  const t = tag(episodeId);
79	  const startedAt = Date.now();
80	
81	  await db
82	    .update(episodes)
83	    .set({ status: "processing" })
84	    .where(eq(episodes.id, episodeId));
85	
86	  const episode = await step(episodeId, "fetch_episode", async () => {
87	    const ep = await fetchEpisodeById(episodeId);
88	    if (!ep) throw new Error("episode not found");
89	    return ep;
90	  });
91	  const preview =
92	    episode.content.length > 70
93	      ? `${episode.content.slice(0, 70)}...`
94	      : episode.content;
95	  console.log(`${t} start "${preview}"`);
96	
97	  const recent = await step(episodeId, "fetch_recent", () =>
98	    getRecentMessages(episode.sessionId, 6, episodeId),
99	  );
100	  console.log(`${t} recent: ${recent.length} prior message(s) loaded`);
101	
102	  const currentMsg = { actor: episode.actor, content: episode.content };
103	
104	  const rawEntities = await step(episodeId, "extract_entities", () =>
105	    extractEntities(currentMsg, recent),
106	  );
107	  console.log(
108	    `${t} extracted ${rawEntities.length} entit${rawEntities.length === 1 ? "y" : "ies"}: ${
109	      rawEntities.length === 0
110	        ? "(none)"
111	        : rawEntities.map((e) => `${e.name}[${e.type}]`).join(", ")
112	    }`,
113	  );
114	
115	  const resolved = await step(episodeId, "resolve_entities", () =>
116	    resolveEntities(episode.userId, rawEntities),
117	  );
118	  const newCount = resolved.filter((r) => r.isNew).length;
119	  console.log(
120	    `${t} resolved ${resolved.length} (${newCount} new): ${
121	      resolved.length === 0
122	        ? "(none)"
123	        : resolved
124	            .map((r) => `${r.name}${r.isNew ? "*" : ""}`)
125	            .join(", ")
126	    }`,
127	  );
128	
129	  const self = await step(episodeId, "get_self_entity", () =>
130	    getOrCreateSelfEntity(episode.userId),
131	  );
132	
133	  const allEntities = [self, ...resolved];
134	
135	  const factsResult = await step(episodeId, "extract_facts", () =>
136	    extractFacts(currentMsg, recent, allEntities),
137	  );
138	  const factsLine =
139	    factsResult.facts.length === 0
140	      ? "(none)"
141	      : factsResult.facts
142	          .map(
143	            (f) =>
144	              `${nameById(allEntities, f.sourceEntityId)}-${f.relationType}->${nameById(allEntities, f.targetEntityId)}`,
145	          )
146	          .join(", ");
147	  const invLine =
148	    factsResult.invalidations.length === 0
149	      ? "(none)"
150	      : factsResult.invalidations
151	          .map(
152	            (i) =>
153	              `${nameById(allEntities, i.sourceEntityId)}-${i.relationType}->${nameById(allEntities, i.targetEntityId)}`,
154	          )
155	          .join(", ");
156	  console.log(`${t} facts (${factsResult.facts.length}): ${factsLine}`);
157	  console.log(
158	    `${t} invalidations (${factsResult.invalidations.length}): ${invLine}`,
159	  );
160	
161	  const occurredAtIso = episode.occurredAt.toISOString();
162	
163	  const temporal = await step(episodeId, "apply_temporal", () =>
164	    applyTemporalFacts(
165	      factsResult.facts,
166	      factsResult.invalidations,
167	      episodeId,
168	      occurredAtIso,
169	    ),
170	  );
171	  console.log(
172	    `${t} temporal: written=${temporal.written.length} closed=${temporal.closed.length} skipped=${temporal.skipped}`,
173	  );
174	
175	  const entVecCount = await step(episodeId, "upsert_entity_vectors", () =>
176	    upsertEntityVectors(resolved),
177	  );
178	  const factVecCount = await step(episodeId, "upsert_fact_vectors", () =>
179	    upsertFactVectors(episode.userId, temporal.written),
180	  );
181	  console.log(
182	    `${t} pinecone: ${entVecCount} entity vector(s), ${factVecCount} fact vector(s) upserted`,
183	  );
184	
185	  await step(episodeId, "create_episode_node", () =>
186	    createEpisodeNode({
187	      episodeId,
188	      userId: episode.userId,
189	      sessionId: episode.sessionId,
190	      actor: episode.actor,
191	      content: episode.content,
192	      occurredAt: occurredAtIso,
193	    }),
194	  );
195	
196	  await step(episodeId, "link_episode_entities", () =>
197	    linkEpisodeToEntities(
198	      episodeId,
199	      allEntities.map((e) => e.entityId),
200	      occurredAtIso,
201	    ),
202	  );
203	  console.log(
204	    `${t} graph: episode node + ${allEntities.length} :MENTIONS edge(s) written`,
205	  );
206	
207	  await db
208	    .update(episodes)
209	    .set({ status: "processed", processedAt: new Date() })
210	    .where(eq(episodes.id, episodeId));
211	
212	  console.log(`${t} done in ${Date.now() - startedAt}ms`);
213	}
214	
215	const worker = new Worker<IngestionJobData>(
216	  INGESTION_QUEUE_NAME,
217	  processEpisode,
218	  {
219	    connection: redisConnection,
220	    concurrency: 4,
221	  },
222	);
223	
224	worker.on("completed", (job) => {
225	  console.log(
226	    `[worker] completed ${job.id} (episode ${job.data.episodeId})`,
227	  );
228	});
229	
230	worker.on("failed", async (job, err) => {
231	  console.error(`[worker] failed ${job?.id}:`, err.message);
232	  if (!job) return;
233	
234	  const isFinalAttempt = job.attemptsMade >= (job.opts.attempts ?? 1);
235	  if (isFinalAttempt) {
236	    await db
237	      .update(episodes)
238	      .set({ status: "failed" })
239	      .where(eq(episodes.id, job.data.episodeId));
240	  }
241	});
242	
243	console.log(`[worker] listening on ${INGESTION_QUEUE_NAME}`);
244
```
</details></tool-use>

4	const ENTITY_TYPES = [
5	  "PERSON",
6	  "COMPANY",
7	  "LOCATION",
8	  "CONCEPT",
9	  "PRODUCT",
10	  "DATE",
11	  "OTHER",
12	] as const;
13	
14	export const RawEntitySchema = z.object({
15	  name: z.string().min(1),
16	  type: z.enum(ENTITY_TYPES),

18	});
19	
20	const EntityListSchema = z.object({

22	});

24	export type RawEntity = z.infer<typeof RawEntitySchema>;

26	export type EpisodeMessage = {
27	  actor: "user" | "assistant" | "system";
28	  content: string;
29	};

31	const ENTITY_EXTRACTION_SYSTEM = `You are an entity extraction system for a long-term memory layer.
32	
33	From the user's CURRENT message, extract every distinct thing worth remembering — people, organizations, places, products, dates, AND concepts the speaker has an opinion, preference, plan, or relationship to (hobbies, things they like/dislike, projects they are building, ideas they hold).
34	
35	For each entity output:
36	- name: canonical form. "Apple Inc." not "apple". "Dogs" not "dogs". Multi-word noun phrases (e.g., "AI memory system", "machine learning model", "neural search engine") are SINGLE entities — do NOT split them into parts. If the current message refers back to something previously discussed using a shortened form ("the project", "it", "the system", "the company"), and the RECENT CONTEXT makes it unambiguous what's being referred to, use the FULL canonical name from the recent context.
37	- type: one of PERSON, COMPANY, LOCATION, CONCEPT, PRODUCT, DATE, OTHER
38	- summary: one short sentence grounded ONLY in what the messages say
39	
40	Rules:
41	- Extract ONLY entities that are referenced in the CURRENT message. DO NOT extract entities that appear in the recent context but are not referenced in the current message.
42	- Do NOT extract the speaker themselves. Their identity is tracked separately by the system.
43	- Do NOT invent entities that aren't in the text.
44	- Bare common nouns the speaker has a relationship to (likes, dislikes, owns, is building, plans, etc.) ARE entities — extract them as CONCEPT or PRODUCT.
45	
46	Examples:
47	
48	Recent context: (none)
49	Input: "I love dogs."
50	Output: { "entities": [ { "name": "Dogs", "type": "CONCEPT", "summary": "Something the speaker loves." } ] }
51	
52	Recent context: (none)
53	Input: "Yesterday I met Karthik at the Anthropic office."
54	Output: { "entities": [
55	  { "name": "Karthik", "type": "PERSON", "summary": "Someone the speaker met yesterday." },
56	  { "name": "Anthropic", "type": "COMPANY", "summary": "Company whose office the speaker visited." }
57	] }
58	
59	Recent context: (none)
60	Input: "I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco."

62	  { "name": "AI memory system", "type": "CONCEPT", "summary": "A project the speaker is building." },
63	  { "name": "Grok", "type": "COMPANY", "summary": "Where the speaker is building the AI memory system." },
64	  { "name": "San Francisco", "type": "LOCATION", "summary": "City where the speaker is based." }
65	] }
66	
67	Recent context:
68	user: I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco.
69	Input: "I'm not building the memory system anymore."
70	Output: { "entities": [
71	  { "name": "AI memory system", "type": "CONCEPT", "summary": "A project the speaker has stopped working on." }
72	] }
73	
74	Recent context:
75	user: I love dogs. I work at Grok.
76	Input: "What's the weather?"
77	Output: { "entities": [] }
78	
79	Return JSON exactly: { "entities": [ { "name": string, "type": string, "summary": string } ] }`;

81	export async function extractEntities(
82	  current: EpisodeMessage,
83	  recent: EpisodeMessage[] = [],
84	): Promise<RawEntity[]> {
85	  const recentBlock = recent.length
86	    ? recent.map((m) => `${m.actor}: ${m.content}`).join("\n")
87	    : "(none)";
88	
89	  const userPrompt = `[Recent context]
90	${recentBlock}
91	
92	[Current message]
93	${current.actor}: ${current.content}`;
94	
95	  const completion = await openai.chat.completions.create({
96	    model: "gpt-4o-mini",
97	    response_format: { type: "json_object" },
98	    messages: [
99	      { role: "system", content: ENTITY_EXTRACTION_SYSTEM },
100	      { role: "user", content: userPrompt },
101	    ],
102	  });
103	
104	  const raw = completion.choices[0]?.message?.content;
105	  if (!raw) return [];
106	
107	  const json = JSON.parse(raw);
108	  const validated = EntityListSchema.parse(json);
109	  return validated.entities;
110	}
111	
112	export type FactExtractionEntity = {
113	  entityId: string;
114	  name: string;
115	  type: string;
116	};
117	
118	export const RawFactSchema = z.object({
119	  sourceEntityId: z.string().min(1),
120	  targetEntityId: z.string().min(1),
121	  relationType: z.string().regex(/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*$/),
122	  factText: z.string().min(1),
123	  confidence: z.number().min(0).max(1),
124	});
125	
126	export const RawInvalidationSchema = z.object({
127	  sourceEntityId: z.string().min(1),
128	  targetEntityId: z.string().min(1),
129	  relationType: z.string().regex(/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*$/),
130	});
131	
132	const FactsResponseSchema = z.object({
133	  facts: z.array(RawFactSchema),
134	  invalidations: z.array(RawInvalidationSchema),
135	});
136	
137	export type RawFact = z.infer<typeof RawFactSchema>;
138	export type RawInvalidation = z.infer<typeof RawInvalidationSchema>;
139	export type FactsExtractionResult = {
140	  facts: RawFact[];
141	  invalidations: RawInvalidation[];
142	};
143	
144	const FACT_EXTRACTION_SYSTEM = `You are a fact extraction system for a temporal knowledge graph.
145	
146	Given a message and a list of entities, extract TWO things:
147	1. NEW relationships between those entities ("facts").
148	2. EXISTING relationships that this message says are now ENDED ("invalidations").
149	
150	Each fact is a triple: source → relation → target.
151	Each invalidation identifies a previously-true triple that should now be considered no longer current.
152	
153	Rules:
154	- Use ONLY the entity IDs from the list below. Do NOT invent entities.
155	- Both source and target of every fact AND every invalidation must be in the list.
156	- The list may include a "User" entity — that represents the speaker. Use it as source for facts/invalidations about the speaker themselves.
157	- relationType: a short verb phrase in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (e.g., WORKS_AT, LIVES_IN, FOUNDED, KNOWS, MET_AT, USED_TO_WORK_AT, LIKES, LOVES, WORKED_ON, BUILDING).
158	- factText: ONE short atomic sentence describing ONLY this triple. Do NOT verbatim-copy the source message — extract just the relationship and write it as a clean standalone fact.
159	- confidence: 0.0 to 1.0 — how strongly the message supports this fact.
160	- Resolve pronouns to their subject. "He used to work..." refers to the previously mentioned PERSON; use that PERSON's entityId as the source, NOT a different entity.
161	- Skip self-loops (source equals target).
162	
163	NEGATION HANDLING (very important):
164	- Do NOT extract negation/cessation as a positive fact. Phrases like "I left X", "I quit X", "I no longer like Y", "X stopped working at Z" must NOT produce facts like LEFT_COMPANY, NO_LONGER_LIKES, STOPPED_WORKING_AT.
165	- Instead, emit an entry in \`invalidations\` naming the previously-true relation that has now ended. Use the canonical positive predicate (e.g., WORKS_AT, LIKES) — NOT a negation.
166	- If the message states both an ending AND a new state ("I left Grok and joined Anthropic"), emit a new fact for the new state AND an invalidation for the old one.
167	
168	Examples:
169	
170	Entities:
171	  e1 → "Kevin" [PERSON]
172	  e2 → "Grok" [COMPANY]
173	Message: "Kevin works at Grok."
174	Output: { "facts": [ { "sourceEntityId": "e1", "targetEntityId": "e2", "relationType": "WORKS_AT", "factText": "Kevin works at Grok.", "confidence": 0.99 } ], "invalidations": [] }
175	
176	Entities:
177	  e1 → "Karthik" [PERSON]
178	  e2 → "OpenAI" [COMPANY]
179	  e3 → "Codex" [PRODUCT]
180	Message: "Karthik used to work at OpenAI on the Codex project."
181	Output: { "facts": [
182	  { "sourceEntityId": "e1", "targetEntityId": "e2", "relationType": "USED_TO_WORK_AT", "factText": "Karthik used to work at OpenAI.", "confidence": 0.99 },
183	  { "sourceEntityId": "e1", "targetEntityId": "e3", "relationType": "WORKED_ON", "factText": "Karthik worked on the Codex project.", "confidence": 0.95 }
184	], "invalidations": [] }
185	
186	Entities:
187	  u1 → "User" [PERSON]
188	  e1 → "Dogs" [CONCEPT]
189	Message: "I love dogs."
190	Output: { "facts": [
191	  { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e1", "relationType": "LOVES", "factText": "The user loves dogs.", "confidence": 0.99 }
192	], "invalidations": [] }
193	
194	Entities:
195	  u1 → "User" [PERSON]
196	  e1 → "Grok" [COMPANY]
197	Message: "I left Grok last month."
198	Output: { "facts": [], "invalidations": [
199	  { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e1", "relationType": "WORKS_AT" }
200	] }
201	
202	Entities:
203	  u1 → "User" [PERSON]
204	  e1 → "Grok" [COMPANY]
205	  e2 → "Anthropic" [COMPANY]
206	Message: "I left Grok and joined Anthropic."
207	Output: { "facts": [
208	  { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e2", "relationType": "WORKS_AT", "factText": "The user works at Anthropic.", "confidence": 0.99 }
209	], "invalidations": [
210	  { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e1", "relationType": "WORKS_AT" }
211	] }
212	
213	Entities:
214	  e1 → "Karthik" [PERSON]
215	  e2 → "Anthropic" [COMPANY]
216	Message: "What's the weather?"
217	Output: { "facts": [], "invalidations": [] }
218	
219	Return JSON exactly: {
220	  "facts": [ { "sourceEntityId": string, "targetEntityId": string, "relationType": string, "factText": string, "confidence": number } ],
221	  "invalidations": [ { "sourceEntityId": string, "targetEntityId": string, "relationType": string } ]
222	}`;
223	
224	export async function extractFacts(
225	  current: EpisodeMessage,
226	  recent: EpisodeMessage[],
227	  entities: FactExtractionEntity[],
228	): Promise<FactsExtractionResult> {
229	  const empty: FactsExtractionResult = { facts: [], invalidations: [] };
230	  if (entities.length < 2) return empty;
231	
232	  const entityList = entities
233	    .map((e) => `  ${e.entityId} → "${e.name}" [${e.type}]`)
234	    .join("\n");
235	
236	  const recentBlock = recent.length
237	    ? recent.map((m) => `${m.actor}: ${m.content}`).join("\n")
238	    : "(none)";
239	
240	  const userPrompt = `Entities:
241	${entityList}
242	
243	[Recent context]
244	${recentBlock}
245	
246	[Current message]
247	${current.actor}: ${current.content}`;
248	
249	  const completion = await openai.chat.completions.create({
250	    model: "gpt-4o-mini",
251	    response_format: { type: "json_object" },
252	    messages: [
253	      { role: "system", content: FACT_EXTRACTION_SYSTEM },
254	      { role: "user", content: userPrompt },
255	    ],
256	  });
257	
258	  const raw = completion.choices[0]?.message?.content;
259	  if (!raw) return empty;
260	
261	  const json = JSON.parse(raw);
262	  const validated = FactsResponseSchema.parse(json);
263	
264	  const validIds = new Set(entities.map((e) => e.entityId));
265	
266	  const facts = validated.facts.filter(
267	    (f) =>
268	      validIds.has(f.sourceEntityId) &&
269	      validIds.has(f.targetEntityId) &&
270	      f.sourceEntityId !== f.targetEntityId,
271	  );
272	
273	  const invalidations = validated.invalidations.filter(
274	    (i) =>
275	      validIds.has(i.sourceEntityId) &&
276	      validIds.has(i.targetEntityId) &&
277	      i.sourceEntityId !== i.targetEntityId,
278	  );
279	
280	  return { facts, invalidations };
281	}

```text
1	import { randomUUID } from "node:crypto";
2	import {
3	  findEntitiesByNormalizedNames,
4	  normalizeEntityName,
5	  upsertEntities,
6	  upsertEntity,
7	  type GraphEntity,
8	} from "./neo4j.service";
9	import type { RawEntity } from "./extraction.service";
10	
11	export type ResolvedEntity = GraphEntity & { isNew: boolean };
12	
13	export async function resolveEntities(
14	  userId: string,
15	  rawEntities: RawEntity[],
16	): Promise<ResolvedEntity[]> {
17	  if (rawEntities.length === 0) return [];
18	
19	  const byNorm = new Map<string, RawEntity>();
20	  for (const raw of rawEntities) {
21	    const norm = normalizeEntityName(raw.name);
22	    if (!byNorm.has(norm)) byNorm.set(norm, raw);
23	  }
24	
25	  const norms = Array.from(byNorm.keys());
26	  const existing = await findEntitiesByNormalizedNames(userId, norms);
27	
28	  const resolved: ResolvedEntity[] = [];
29	  const newEntities: GraphEntity[] = [];
30	
31	  for (const [norm, raw] of byNorm) {
32	    const hit = existing.get(norm);
33	    if (hit) {
34	      resolved.push({ ...hit, isNew: false });
35	      continue;
36	    }
37	    const entity: GraphEntity = {
38	      entityId: randomUUID(),
39	      userId,
40	      name: raw.name,
41	      normalizedName: norm,
42	      type: raw.type,
43	      summary: raw.summary,
44	    };
45	    newEntities.push(entity);
46	    resolved.push({ ...entity, isNew: true });
47	  }
48	
49	  if (newEntities.length > 0) {
50	    await upsertEntities(newEntities);
51	  }
52	
53	  return resolved;
54	}
55	
56	export async function getOrCreateSelfEntity(
57	  userId: string,
58	): Promise<ResolvedEntity> {
59	  const entity: GraphEntity = {
60	    entityId: userId,
61	    userId,
62	    name: "User",
63	    normalizedName: "user",
64	    type: "PERSON",
65	    summary: "The user (speaker).",
66	  };
67	  await upsertEntity(entity);
68	  return { ...entity, isNew: false };
69	}
70

 `./server/src/services/temporal.service.ts`

```text
1	import { randomUUID } from "node:crypto";
2	import {
3	  closeFact,
4	  findOpenFactsForSourceAndRelation,
5	  upsertFact,
6	  type GraphFact,
7	} from "./neo4j.service";
8	import type { RawFact, RawInvalidation } from "./extraction.service";
9	
10	export type TemporalFact = GraphFact & { isNew: boolean };
11	
12	export type ClosedEdge = {
13	  factId: string;
14	  targetEntityId: string;
15	  closedAt: string;
16	};
17	
18	export type TemporalResult = {
19	  written: TemporalFact[];
20	  closed: ClosedEdge[];
21	  skipped: number;
22	};
23	
24	export async function applyTemporalFacts(
25	  rawFacts: RawFact[],
26	  invalidations: RawInvalidation[],
27	  episodeId: string,
28	  occurredAt: string,
29	): Promise<TemporalResult> {
30	  if (rawFacts.length === 0 && invalidations.length === 0) {
31	    return { written: [], closed: [], skipped: 0 };
32	  }
33	
34	  const groupKey = (sourceId: string, relation: string) =>
35	    `${sourceId}|${relation}`;
36	
37	  const uniqueGroups = new Map<string, { sourceId: string; relation: string }>();
38	  for (const f of rawFacts) {
39	    const key = groupKey(f.sourceEntityId, f.relationType);
40	    if (!uniqueGroups.has(key)) {
41	      uniqueGroups.set(key, {
42	        sourceId: f.sourceEntityId,
43	        relation: f.relationType,
44	      });
45	    }
46	  }
47	
48	  const existingByGroup = new Map<
49	    string,
50	    Array<{ factId: string; targetEntityId: string }>
51	  >();
52	  await Promise.all(
53	    Array.from(uniqueGroups.entries()).map(async ([key, { sourceId, relation }]) => {
54	      const opens = await findOpenFactsForSourceAndRelation(sourceId, relation);
55	      existingByGroup.set(key, opens);
56	    }),
57	  );
58	
59	  const newTargetsByGroup = new Map<string, Set<string>>();
60	  for (const f of rawFacts) {
61	    const key = groupKey(f.sourceEntityId, f.relationType);
62	    if (!newTargetsByGroup.has(key)) newTargetsByGroup.set(key, new Set());
63	    newTargetsByGroup.get(key)!.add(f.targetEntityId);
64	  }
65	
66	  const toClose: Array<{ factId: string; targetEntityId: string }> = [];
67	  for (const [key, existings] of existingByGroup) {
68	    const newTargets = newTargetsByGroup.get(key) ?? new Set<string>();
69	    for (const old of existings) {
70	      if (!newTargets.has(old.targetEntityId)) {
71	        toClose.push(old);
72	      }
73	    }
74	  }
75	
76	  await Promise.all(
77	    toClose.map((edge) => closeFact(edge.factId, occurredAt)),
78	  );
79	  const closed: ClosedEdge[] = toClose.map((edge) => ({
80	    factId: edge.factId,
81	    targetEntityId: edge.targetEntityId,
82	    closedAt: occurredAt,
83	  }));
84	
85	  const written: TemporalFact[] = [];
86	  let skipped = 0;
87	
88	  const writes: Promise<void>[] = [];
89	  for (const raw of rawFacts) {
90	    const key = groupKey(raw.sourceEntityId, raw.relationType);
91	    const existings = existingByGroup.get(key) ?? [];
92	    const reaffirmation = existings.find(
93	      (e) => e.targetEntityId === raw.targetEntityId,
94	    );
95	    if (reaffirmation) {
96	      skipped++;
97	      continue;
98	    }
99	
100	    const fact: GraphFact = {
101	      factId: randomUUID(),
102	      sourceEntityId: raw.sourceEntityId,
103	      targetEntityId: raw.targetEntityId,
104	      relationType: raw.relationType,
105	      factText: raw.factText,
106	      validFrom: occurredAt,
107	      validUntil: null,
108	      confidence: raw.confidence,
109	      episodeId,
110	    };
111	    writes.push(upsertFact(fact));
112	    written.push({ ...fact, isNew: true });
113	  }
114	  await Promise.all(writes);
115	
116	  // Explicit invalidations: close any open edge matching (source, relationType, target).
117	  // Idempotent: edges already closed by contradiction logic above are simply re-stamped
118	  // with the same `validUntil` (no-op).
119	  const invalidationGroups = new Map<
120	    string,
121	    Array<{ factId: string; targetEntityId: string }>
122	  >();
123	  await Promise.all(
124	    invalidations.map(async (inv) => {
125	      const key = groupKey(inv.sourceEntityId, inv.relationType);
126	      if (invalidationGroups.has(key)) return;
127	      const opens =
128	        existingByGroup.get(key) ??
129	        (await findOpenFactsForSourceAndRelation(
130	          inv.sourceEntityId,
131	          inv.relationType,
132	        ));
133	      invalidationGroups.set(key, opens);
134	    }),
135	  );
136	
137	  const invalidationCloses: ClosedEdge[] = [];
138	  await Promise.all(
139	    invalidations.map(async (inv) => {
140	      const key = groupKey(inv.sourceEntityId, inv.relationType);
141	      const opens = invalidationGroups.get(key) ?? [];
142	      const matching = opens.filter(
143	        (e) => e.targetEntityId === inv.targetEntityId,
144	      );
145	      for (const m of matching) {
146	        await closeFact(m.factId, occurredAt);
147	        invalidationCloses.push({
148	          factId: m.factId,
149	          targetEntityId: m.targetEntityId,
150	          closedAt: occurredAt,
151	        });
152	      }
153	    }),
154	  );
155	
156	  return {
157	    written,
158	    closed: [...closed, ...invalidationCloses],
159	    skipped,
160	  };
161	}
162
```
</details></tool-use>
