_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:33:24Z)**_

Fixes 1+2 didn't break anything (22/22 still green). They DO help — Q4 in the second scenario now correctly answers "You are not currently building the AI memory system anymore", which is a real improvement from the prompt sharpening.

But the Cats-Dogs symptom is a **concurrency bug, not an LLM bug**. The cheapest robust fix is one extra line in `closeFact`: refuse to close an edge whose `validFrom` is later than the proposed `validUntil`. That makes the operation idempotent under out-of-order processing.

Want me to apply that one-line Cypher guard? It's strictly defensive and changes no other behavior:

```cypher
MATCH ()-[f:FACT { factId: $factId }]->()
WHERE f.validFrom <= datetime($validUntil)    ← new
SET f.validUntil = datetime($validUntil),
    f.tExpired = coalesce(f.tExpired, datetime())
```

With this in place: when ep1 tries to close Cats at `2026-05-07`, the `WHERE` filters Cats out (its `validFrom=2026-05-08` is later), the SET never runs, and Cats stays open. Dogs would still get closed by ep2's explicit invalidation as designed.

Alternatively, the structural fix is to set the BullMQ worker `concurrency: 1` (or one-per-user via a job group) — both are also simple but change throughput characteristics. Your call.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:41:20Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:42:14Z)**_

Here's the whole ingestion path in plain words, in the exact order Postman will hit it.

---

## 0. The 30-second mental model

You POST a message. The server **immediately** writes a row to Postgres, drops a job on a Redis queue, and returns `202 Accepted` with an episode ID. Your Postman request comes back in milliseconds — **no LLM work happened in your request**.

Then a separate background worker picks up the job, does all the slow stuff (extract entities, extract facts, figure out time, write to the graph + vector store), and updates the episode's status from `queued` → `processing` → `processed`. You can poll `GET /episodes/:id` to watch this happen.

Three datastores end up holding the result:
- **Postgres** — the raw message, audit logs.
- **Neo4j** — entities, facts, and the temporal graph that ties them together.
- **Pinecone** — vectors so semantic search can find them later.

---

## 1. Before you ingest (Postman setup, do once)

**Sign up** — gives you a JWT.
```
POST http://localhost:3002/auth/signup
content-type: application/json

{ "email": "me@x.com", "password": "hunter22hunter22" }
```
Response → save `token` into a Postman variable.

**Create a session** — basically a chat thread to group messages.
```
POST http://localhost:3002/sessions
authorization: Bearer {{token}}
content-type: application/json

{ "title": "test" }
```
Response → save `session.id` as `sessionId`.

These two are one-time per test run.

---

## 2. The actual ingest call

```
POST http://localhost:3002/ingest
authorization: Bearer {{token}}
content-type: application/json

{
  "sessionId": "{{sessionId}}",
  "actor": "user",
  "content": "I love dogs. I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco.",
  "occurredAt": "2026-05-07T10:00:00.000Z",
  "type": "message"
}
```

**`actor`** is `"user"` | `"assistant"` | `"system"` — tells the system who said it.
**`occurredAt`** is optional. If you omit it, the server uses "now". I'd send it explicitly so different messages have different timestamps (so the temporal logic has something to work with).
**`type`** is optional, defaults to `"message"`. The other options are `"text"` and `"json"` — they're accepted but right now the worker treats all three the same (this is one of the things I added).

**Response — comes back almost instantly:**
```json
{ "episodeId": "936e2116-…", "status": "queued" }
```
Save `episodeId`. You'll use it to check progress.

---

## 3. What the worker does, in order, in plain words

The moment the job lands in the queue, a Bun worker process picks it up. Concurrency is 4 — meaning up to 4 episodes can be processed at the same time. Each step writes a row to `processing_logs` so you can debug.

### Step 1 — "I see this job, let me mark it"
Sets `episodes.status = "processing"` so anyone looking at Postgres knows it's in flight.

### Step 2 — "Let me re-read the message and its recent context"
Loads the episode row from Postgres + the previous 6 messages in the same session. The previous messages give the LLM the context to handle things like pronouns ("he", "it", "the project") and to know what the speaker was talking about earlier.

### Step 3 — "What real-world things does this message talk about?"
Calls OpenAI (gpt-4o-mini) with a fixed prompt that says "extract every named thing AND every concept the speaker has a relationship to."

For `"I love dogs. I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco."` it returns roughly:
- `Dogs` [CONCEPT]
- `AI memory system` [CONCEPT]
- `Grok` [COMPANY]
- `San Francisco` [LOCATION]

Note it does NOT extract the user themselves — the system tracks the speaker as a special `User` entity automatically.

> **🆕 New bit (flag `ZEP_LLM_REFLEXION=1`):** After the first extraction, a second LLM call reviews the list and says "you missed X" or "Y shouldn't be here". Then we merge in the corrections. This costs an extra LLM call per message but catches subtle things the first pass misses (e.g. "I've been getting into bouldering" — first pass might miss "Bouldering" as a CONCEPT, critic catches it).

### Step 4 — "Have we seen these entities before? If yes, reuse the same node."
For each entity name, we normalize it (`"Apple Inc."` → `"apple inc."`) and ask Neo4j: "do you already have an entity for this user with this normalized name?"
- **Match** → reuse the existing `entityId`. Marked as `isNew: false`.
- **No match** → mint a new UUID, create a new `:Entity` node in Neo4j. Marked as `isNew: true`.

> **🆕 New bit (flag `ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1`):** If the name doesn't match exactly, we go semantic: embed the name+summary, search Pinecone for similar existing entities, and ask the LLM "is this 'Anthropic Inc.' the same as your existing 'Anthropic'?" If yes, reuse the existing ID. This stops the graph from getting cluttered with near-duplicates.

### Step 5 — "Make sure the speaker is registered as an entity"
Idempotently upserts a `User` entity whose ID equals your userId. So `User` is always available as the source of facts about the speaker.

### Step 6 — "What relationships does this message create?"
Another OpenAI call. Now the prompt has the list of entities (including `User`) and asks for two things:
- **`facts[]`** — new positive relationships. e.g. `User -[LOVES]-> Dogs`, `User -[BUILDING]-> AI memory system`, `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Grok`, `User -[LIVES_IN]-> San Francisco`.
- **`invalidations[]`** — relationships the message says have ENDED. e.g. for `"I'm not building the memory system anymore."` you'd get `{source: User, target: AI memory system, relationType: BUILDING}`.

The prompt is strict about negation: `"I left Grok"` does NOT become a positive fact `LEFT_AT`; it becomes an invalidation of `WORKS_AT`. This is what makes contradiction detection clean later.

### Step 7 — "Write the facts to the graph, handling time and contradictions"
This is the most complex step. Several things happen in order inside `applyTemporalFacts`:

**7a. Figure out when each fact was true (event time).**
By default, `validFrom = occurredAt` (the timestamp of the message).

> **🆕 New bit (flag `ZEP_LLM_TEMPORAL=1`):** Call the LLM per fact with prompt "given the fact text and a reference timestamp, when did this start and (if mentioned) end?". So `"two years ago"` resolves to `occurredAt - 2 years`. `"yesterday"` resolves to `occurredAt - 1 day`. If the LLM returns nulls, we fall back to `occurredAt`. There's a defensive guard: if the LLM says "ended before it started", we ignore that and keep the edge open.

**7b. Rule-based contradiction closure.**
For each new fact, look at existing OPEN edges with the same `(source, relationType)` but a different target. Example: existing `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Grok` is open. New fact says `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Anthropic`. We close the Grok edge by setting `validUntil = occurredAt`. The Grok edge is preserved (not deleted) — that's the temporal-graph magic. You can later ask "what did the user believe in March?" vs "what's true now?".

**7c. Write each new fact.** Three sub-cases:
- **Reaffirmation** (the new fact triple `(src, rel, tgt)` already exists open): append the new episode ID to that edge's `episodeIds` list, bump confidence by 0.01, update `lastSeenAt`. Don't write a duplicate edge. 🆕 *This used to be "skip silently"; now the edge accumulates provenance across messages.*
- 🆕 **Pair-scoped semantic dedup** (flag `ZEP_LLM_FACT_DEDUP=1`): before writing, check if an existing edge between the same `(source, target)` pair has semantically-similar text. e.g. "user works at Anthropic" vs "user is employed by Anthropic" — same fact, different label. If cosine ≥ 0.85, treat as reaffirmation.
- **New edge** (default): write a fresh `:FACT` edge with `factId`, `validFrom`, `validUntil`, 🆕 `tCreated = now()` (transaction time — when we LEARNED), 🆕 `episodeIds = [thisEpisode]`, `confidence`, `factText`.

**7d. Explicit invalidations.**
For each entry in `invalidations[]` from Step 6, find the matching open edge and close it. That's how `"I'm not building the memory system anymore."` closes the existing `BUILDING` edge. The closure sets `validUntil = occurredAt` AND 🆕 `tExpired = now()`.

> **🆕 New bit (flag `ZEP_LLM_INVALIDATE=1`):** After all the rule-based closures, we look at remaining open edges from the same source, embed them, find semantically-similar ones, and ask the LLM "does the new fact contradict this old one?". Catches cross-predicate contradictions the rule path misses, e.g. `LIVES_IN Boston` vs new `RESIDES_IN Tokyo`.

### Step 8 — "Push new entity + fact vectors to Pinecone"
- For each NEW entity: embed `name + summary` with OpenAI, upsert to Pinecone with metadata `{type: "entity", userId, name, entityType}`.
- For each new fact: embed `factText`, upsert with metadata `{type: "fact", userId, sourceId, targetId, relationType, factText, ` 🆕 `validFromMs, validUntilMs, isOpen}`. The new temporal fields let retrieval filter by time at the vector layer.

### Step 9 — "Create the Episode node and link it to entities"
- Create `(:Episode {episodeId, content, actor, occurredAt})` in Neo4j.
- For each entity in this message (including the User), create `(ep)-[:MENTIONS]->(entity)`. This is what lets you later trace any fact back to the messages that produced it.

### Step 10 — "Done"
Set `episodes.status = "processed"`, `episodes.processedAt = now()`.

---

## 4. What the new bits give you, in one paragraph each

**Tier 1 (always on, no flag needed):**
- **`tCreated` and `tExpired` on every fact edge** — paper calls this "transaction time". Tells you WHEN THE SYSTEM LEARNED a fact, separately from WHEN IT WAS TRUE. So you can ask "what did the system think yesterday?" — useful for audits and debugging.
- **`episodeIds: [...]` list on every fact edge** — every episode that asserted this fact gets added. Lets you cite sources ("the user said this in messages X, Y, Z").
- **Pinecone fact vectors carry `validFromMs`/`isOpen`** — retrieval can filter "currently true" without touching Neo4j.
- **`type: "message"|"text"|"json"`** — accepted on the API and stored in `metadata`. Forward-compatible for non-conversational ingestion.
- **Reaffirmation now bumps confidence + provenance** — saying the same thing twice strengthens the edge instead of being silently dropped.

**Tier 2 (turn on with `.env.local`):**
- **`ZEP_LLM_TEMPORAL=1`** — relative dates like "two years ago" actually resolve correctly.
- **`ZEP_LLM_REFLEXION=1`** — entity extraction gets a critic pass; fewer misses and hallucinations.
- **`ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1`** — "Apple" and "Apple Inc." merge into one entity instead of two.
- **`ZEP_LLM_INVALIDATE=1`** — catches contradictions across different relationType labels.
- **`ZEP_LLM_FACT_DEDUP=1`** — `WORKS_AT` and `EMPLOYED_BY` stop becoming duplicate edges.

Each flag is independent. With all of them off, the system behaves like the rule-based baseline. Each flag adds one or more LLM calls per message.

---

## 5. How to verify a result from Postman

**Watch a single episode finish:**
```
GET http://localhost:3002/episodes/{{episodeId}}
authorization: Bearer {{token}}
```
Response includes `episode.status` (`queued | processing | processed | failed`) and a `logs[]` array — one entry per step, with `duration_ms` and `message` (the error message if it failed). Poll this every 1–2 seconds; it usually takes 3–8 seconds with all flags on.

**See the whole graph for your user (a debug-only endpoint):**
```
GET http://localhost:3002/graph
authorization: Bearer {{token}}
```
Returns `entities[]`, `facts[]` (with `validFrom`, `validUntil`, `relationType`, etc.), `episodes[]`, `mentions[]`. Best way to confirm a fact actually got written with the timestamps you expect.

**Query the memory:**
```
POST http://localhost:3002/retrieve
authorization: Bearer {{token}}
content-type: application/json

{ "query": "what does the user love", "sessionId": "{{sessionId}}", "limit": 6 }
```
Response has `answer` (LLM-synthesized one-liner), `facts` (the top-ranked supporting facts with scores), `context` (formatted text that an agent would paste into its prompt), and `recentMessages`.

---

## 6. Failure modes to watch for in Postman

| You see | Means |
|---|---|
| `404 SESSION_NOT_FOUND` | Wrong `sessionId` or it belongs to a different user. |
| `400 VALIDATION_ERROR` | Body doesn't match the schema (missing `actor`, etc.). Check `issues[]` in response. |
| `401` | JWT missing or expired. Re-signin. |
| `episode.status = "failed"` after a while | The worker hit an error. Look at `logs[]` for the step that errored. The most common one is OpenAI rate limit / invalid key. |
| Episode stuck at `queued` for >30 seconds | The worker isn't running. Check the worker terminal. |

---

## 7. Practical testing tips

- **Ingest one message at a time and wait for it to process before sending the next**, especially while testing. The worker has `concurrency: 4` — if you fire five messages back-to-back, they process in parallel, and the temporal ordering can get scrambled (we saw this in the scenario test). For ordered behavior, space them 5+ seconds apart.
- **Send `occurredAt` explicitly.** It makes the "two days ago" / "last week" tests reproducible and protects against clock-skew weirdness.
- **The flags are read by the worker, not the server.** If you change `.env.local`, restart the worker process.
- **`bun run verify`** from your terminal walks Postgres + Neo4j + Pinecone for the most-recently-active user and prints everything. Run it after a Postman session to see the actual graph state.

That's the whole picture. Send your first message, watch `GET /episodes/:id` flip from `queued` to `processed`, then `GET /graph` to see what your message turned into.

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:59:59Z)**_

Looking at your worker log and your Pinecone screenshot — your 8 records are exactly **4 entity vectors + 4 fact vectors**, which is the textbook outcome of one ingest. Here's the rule for each type.

## Entity vectors

**Created exactly when:** the resolver decides an entity is brand new for this user (`isNew: true`).

That happens in **Step 4** of the worker (`resolve_entities`):
- Run normalized-name lookup against Neo4j for this `userId`.
- If a row comes back → it's an existing entity, `isNew: false`, **no new vector.**
- If no row → mint a fresh UUID, mark `isNew: true`, **upsert vector to Pinecone.**

With the flag `ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1`:
- If the semantic hybrid path finds a match in Pinecone → reuse existing, `isNew: false`, **no new vector.**
- Only if it doesn't find a match does it fall through to "create new" and a vector is written.

**What goes into the vector:**
- Vector content = embedding of `"${name}: ${summary}"` (so the name AND the one-line summary contribute).
- Metadata: `type: "entity"`, `userId`, `neo4jId` (= the entityId), `name`, `entityType` (PERSON / COMPANY / LOCATION / CONCEPT / PRODUCT / DATE / OTHER).

**One important exception** that explains your screenshot: the `User`/self entity is **never sent to Pinecone**. The worker calls `upsertEntityVectors(resolved)` — `resolved` does not include the self entity, only the entities extracted from the message. That's why you see `Dogs / AI memory system / Grok / San Francisco` (4) but not `User`. The User entity exists in Neo4j (you'd see 5 entities in `bun run verify`) but is intentionally not vectorized — it's matched implicitly through the facts that point to it.

**Frequency:** exactly once per entity per user, the first time the user mentions it. After that, every subsequent message that mentions the same entity reuses the existing vector — no re-upsert.

## Fact vectors

**Created exactly when:** the temporal step writes a NEW `:FACT` edge to Neo4j — i.e. the fact appears in `temporal.written`.

The temporal step has three outcomes per extracted fact, and only one creates a vector:

| Outcome | Neo4j action | Pinecone action |
|---|---|---|
| **New fact** (the triple is new) | Create a new `:FACT` edge with a fresh `factId` | ✅ **Upsert a new fact vector** |
| **Reaffirmation** (same `source + relationType + target` already open) | `reinforceFact` — append episode ID, bump confidence, update `lastSeenAt` | ❌ No new vector — the existing one still represents this fact |
| **Pair-scoped dedup match** (flag 2.9, semantically same fact, different relationType label) | `reinforceFact` on the existing edge | ❌ No new vector |
| **Closure / invalidation** (an existing edge gets `validUntil` set) | `closeFact` — sets `validUntil` and `tExpired` | ❌ No new vector, and the **existing vector's `isOpen` metadata is NOT updated** (more on this below) |

**What goes into the vector:**
- Vector content = embedding of `factText` only (e.g. `"The user loves dogs."`). The source/target names are NOT part of the embedding text.
- Metadata: `type: "fact"`, `userId`, `neo4jId` (= factId), `sourceId`, `targetId`, `relationType`, `factText`, `validFromMs`, `isOpen`. If the fact arrived already closed (e.g. LLM temporal extraction returned an `invalidAt`), it also has `validUntilMs`.

**Your 4 fact vectors come from:**
| Edge | factText |
|---|---|
| `User-LOVES-Dogs` | "The user loves dogs." |
| `User-BUILDING-AI memory system` | "The user is building an AI memory system." |
| `User-WORKS_AT-Grok` | "The user works at Grok." |
| `Grok-LOCATED_IN-San Francisco` | "Grok is located in San Francisco." |

All four have `isOpen: true` and `validFromMs: 1778601380838` (= 2026-05-12T..., the `occurredAt` of your ingest — you didn't pass an explicit `occurredAt` so the server used "now").

## A subtle wart you should know about

When a fact edge gets **closed later** (e.g. you send "I'm not building the memory system anymore"), the worker:
- Updates Neo4j: sets `validUntil` and `tExpired` on the edge. ✅
- **Does NOT update Pinecone.** The vector's `isOpen` metadata stays `true` even though the underlying fact is now closed.

This matters if you ever filter Pinecone by `isOpen: true` directly — you'll get stale hits. The retrieval pipeline today doesn't rely on that metadata for correctness (it does the final temporal filter in Neo4j via `expandFromIds`), so it's not a bug *yet*, but it's a footgun.

If you want it fixed, the change is small: after closures in the temporal step, re-upsert the closed fact vectors with the new `validUntilMs` and `isOpen: false`. One-liner in the worker. Don't need to do anything now — just flagging it since you're staring at the Pinecone state.

## Mental model in one sentence

> One entity vector is born the first time you see a new entity (except User, which never gets one). One fact vector is born every time a brand-new `:FACT` edge is written. Reaffirmations, dedups, and closures never create vectors and never update existing ones.

That's the whole rule.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:00:53Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:00:58Z)**_

Yes, it's better. Three real reasons:

1. **Truth-in-metadata.** Right now Pinecone says `isOpen: true` for a fact Neo4j has already closed. That's just wrong, and anyone reading Pinecone directly (you, a dashboard, a future filter) will be misled.

2. **Lets retrieval prune cheaply.** The whole point of Tier 1.4 (mirroring temporal fields on the vector) was so retrieval can filter "currently true" facts at the vector layer instead of always round-tripping to Neo4j. If `isOpen` lies, that optimization is unusable — every query has to fall back to the graph hop to verify. Fixing the update makes the metadata actually load-bearing.

3. **It's nearly free.** Pinecone has a metadata-only `update` API — you don't re-embed, you just send `{ id, metadata: { isOpen: false, validUntilMs: ... } }`. One extra small request per closed fact, max one per ingest in 95% of cases.

The only honest argument against is "more code paths, more failure modes" — but the failure mode (Pinecone stale relative to Neo4j) is **already what we have today**. Updating just moves the staleness from "permanent" to "transient on error", which is strictly better.

Verdict: ship the fix.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:03:00Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:03:15Z)**_

You're seeing two **different kinds of question** the system needs to answer — entity vectors and fact vectors solve them separately, then the retrieval code merges the results.

## Entity vector = "this is a THING"

```
id: 18f9ff48...      ← entityId, same id used in Neo4j
type: "entity"
name: "San Francisco"
entityType: "LOCATION"
```

Embedded text: `"San Francisco: <summary>"` — just the name plus the one-line summary. It says nothing about what San Francisco is doing or who relates to it.

This is what catches queries that mention a thing by name:
- "Tell me about San Francisco"
- "Who is Karthik"
- "My friend in Japan" → matches the `Tokyo` entity → graph hop finds Mei

Without entity vectors, those queries have no anchor — there's no relationship-shaped fact text saying "San Francisco is a city" for the query to hit.

## Fact vector = "this is a RELATIONSHIP between two things"

```
id: b068d97b...      ← factId, same id used in Neo4j
type: "fact"
factText: "Grok is located in San Francisco."
relationType: "LOCATED_IN"
sourceId:  52010220...   (Grok entity)
targetId:  18f9ff48...   (San Francisco entity)
isOpen: true
validFromMs: 1778544000000
```

Embedded text: `"Grok is located in San Francisco."` — just the propositional sentence. The name "San Francisco" only appears as part of a relationship.

This is what catches queries that key on the relationship itself, not the entities:
- "Where is Grok located" → matches this fact vector directly
- "Where does the user work" → matches `"The user works at Grok"` fact vector
- "What does the user love" → matches `"The user loves dogs"` fact vector

A user asking "where does the user work" doesn't mention "Grok" by name — there's no entity vector for that query to hit. The fact vector is what carries the answer.

## Why both lanes are necessary

| Query shape | What it hits | Why |
|---|---|---|
| "Tell me about San Francisco" | Entity vector for SF | Query mentions an entity by name. Strong cosine on the entity's name+summary embedding. |
| "Where does the user work" | Fact vector for `User-WORKS_AT-Grok` | No specific named entity in the query — it's a relationship question. The fact's `factText` carries the answer. |
| "Anything connected to Karthik" | Entity vector for Karthik → graph hops to all his facts | Entity hit gives an anchor; graph traversal does the rest. |
| "Did the user use to work at OpenAI" | Both — fact text matches, entity name matches | Multi-prong recall. |

The retrieval code in `retrieval.service.ts` makes this explicit:

```ts
for (const m of matches) {
  if (m.type === "entity") entityIds.push(m.id);
  else if (m.type === "fact") factIds.push(m.id);
}
const expanded = await expandFromIds(userId, { entityIds, factIds, asOf });
```

After Pinecone returns mixed matches, it splits them. Fact IDs go directly to the answer set (`hop=0`). Entity IDs go through a 1-hop graph traversal in Neo4j to pull all facts attached to that entity (`hop=1`). Both streams converge on facts — which is what the LLM actually needs to answer.

## Why this two-vector design exists (paper §3.1)

This is the paper's "multi-faceted candidate identification": the three search lanes — full-text, cosine over names, cosine over fact text — each find different things, and together they maximise the chance of pulling the right facts into the candidate pool. Your implementation does only the cosine lanes (not BM25), but it's the same idea: **different vector types catch different question shapes**.

## In your concrete state

You have:
- **4 entity vectors** (`Dogs`, `AI memory system`, `Grok`, `San Francisco`) — each anchors queries that name the thing.
- **4 fact vectors** (`User-LOVES-Dogs`, `User-BUILDING-AI memory system`, `User-WORKS_AT-Grok`, `Grok-LOCATED_IN-San Francisco`) — each anchors queries that name the relationship.

Same world, two views. Entities tell you *what exists*. Facts tell you *what those things are doing with each other*. The graph (Neo4j) is the source of truth that ties them together by `entityId` / `factId`; Pinecone is the recall layer that lets a free-text query find the relevant ones.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:04:22Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:04:33Z)**_

Both vectors are written in **Step 8** of the worker, but they're sourced from two different earlier steps. Here's the data flow:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 4: resolve_entities                │
│   → resolved[] (each has isNew flag)    │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │   only items with isNew:true
                   ▼
       ┌───────────────────────────┐
       │ Step 8a: upsert_entity_   │
       │           vectors          │
       │  embeds "${name}: ${summary}"│
       │  → ENTITY vectors in       │
       │     Pinecone               │
       └───────────────────────────┘


┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 7: apply_temporal                  │
│   → temporal.written[] (new :FACT edges)│
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │   all written facts
                   ▼
       ┌───────────────────────────┐
       │ Step 8b: upsert_fact_     │
       │           vectors          │
       │  embeds factText only      │
       │  → FACT vectors in         │
       │     Pinecone               │
       └───────────────────────────┘
```

In the actual worker code:

```ts
// from workers/ingestion.worker.ts
const entVecCount = await step(episodeId, "upsert_entity_vectors", () =>
  upsertEntityVectors(resolved),          //  ← entity vectors  (Step 8a)
);
const factVecCount = await step(episodeId, "upsert_fact_vectors", () =>
  upsertFactVectors(episode.userId, temporal.written),   //  ← fact vectors  (Step 8b)
);
```

Looking at your worker log, you'll see this exact line — that's Step 8 firing:

```
[0d0f099f] pinecone: 4 entity vector(s), 4 fact vector(s) upserted
```

The two `step(...)` wrappers also emit `processing_logs` rows so you can see them per episode if you check Postgres:

```sql
SELECT step, status, duration_ms
FROM processing_logs
WHERE episode_id = '0d0f099f-...'
ORDER BY created_at;
```

You'll see `upsert_entity_vectors` and `upsert_fact_vectors` as two separate steps, each with their own duration. The entity step is usually faster (only new entities get embedded; the second time you mention "Grok", nothing happens here). The fact step embeds every new fact.

## Where each step's INPUT comes from

| Vector type | Sourced from | Filter applied before upsert |
|---|---|---|
| **Entity** | `resolved` array from Step 4 (`resolve_entities`) | Only entries with `isNew === true` — i.e. entities that didn't already exist in Neo4j for this user. |
| **Fact** | `temporal.written` array from Step 7 (`apply_temporal`) | All of them — but note that reaffirmations and pair-dedup matches never land in `written` in the first place, so they're filtered out upstream by `applyTemporalFacts`. |

So:
- **Step 4 decides which entities become vectors.** If your entity already exists for this user, it never reaches the entity-upsert step.
- **Step 7 decides which facts become vectors.** Reaffirmations and merges into existing edges never reach the fact-upsert step — they go through `reinforceFact` (Neo4j only, no Pinecone write).

That's the full answer. **Step 4 → Step 8a** for entity vectors, **Step 7 → Step 8b** for fact vectors.

cd /Users/karthiknadar/desktop/zep-paper-implementation/server && bun -e '
import { desc } from "drizzle-orm";
import { db } from "./src/models/db";
import { episodes } from "./src/models/schema";

  .from(episodes).orderBy(desc(episodes.createdAt)).limit(1);

console.log("userId:", userId);

// Find CLOSED facts in Neo4j for this user

const r = await s.run(`
  MATCH (a:Entity {userId: $userId})-[f:FACT]->(b:Entity)
  WHERE f.validUntil IS NOT NULL
  RETURN f.factId AS factId,
         a.name AS src, f.relationType AS rel, b.name AS tgt,
         toString(f.validUntil) AS validUntil
`, { userId });
await s.close();
await neo4jDriver.close();

}

const recs = fetch.records ?? {};

  console.log(`  ${id.slice(0,8)}..  isOpen=${isOpen}  validUntilMs=${vu}  (${vuISO})`);
}
' 2>&1

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:12:10Z)**_

## What shipped

Two files changed:

**`server/src/services/pinecone.service.ts`** — added `markFactVectorsClosed(closures)`. Uses Pinecone's metadata-only `index.update({ id, metadata })` API so no re-embedding cost. Fail-soft: a Pinecone error logs and continues without aborting the ingestion (Neo4j stays consistent regardless).

**`server/src/workers/ingestion.worker.ts`** — added one new step after `upsert_fact_vectors`:
```ts
const closedVecCount = await step(
  episodeId, "update_closed_fact_vectors",
  () => markFactVectorsClosed(temporal.closed),
);
```
The worker log now reports it inline:
```
[abc12345] pinecone: 4 entity vector(s), 4 fact vector(s) upserted, 1 closed
```

And it appears as its own row in `processing_logs` so you can see latency per episode.

## Behavior, in one paragraph

When `applyTemporalFacts` closes an edge — whether by rule-based contradiction, explicit LLM invalidation, or semantic invalidation under `ZEP_LLM_INVALIDATE=1` — the worker now also patches that fact's Pinecone metadata. `isOpen` flips to `false` and `validUntilMs` is set to the same `closedAt` timestamp Neo4j received. Reaffirmations and dedup merges still write nothing to Pinecone (correct — no new facts, no closures). Pure-creation episodes (your message 1) will now show `0 closed` in the worker log because nothing got closed.

## Confirmed

- TypeScript clean (`bunx tsc --noEmit`).
- `test:temporal` 21/21 still pass (in-process tests don't touch Pinecone but confirm I didn't break the temporal pipeline).
- The new step is wired before any later step that could fail, so if Pinecone is down, the rest of the pipeline still completes.

Restart the worker and you're good. Want me to also write a quick `test:pinecone-closure` script that exercises the round-trip (upsert open fact → close it → fetch back from Pinecone → assert `isOpen: false`)?

# The ingestion pipeline, end to end, from scratch

A complete walkthrough including every change we've added. I'll structure this so each piece is anchored to a file and a reason. The pipeline ingests one message at a time; what follows is what happens to ONE message between the moment your Postman request lands and the moment its derived facts can be retrieved.

---

## 0. The big picture in one paragraph

You POST a chat message to `/ingest`. The server writes a row to Postgres, drops a job on a Redis queue, and returns `202` immediately — your client doesn't wait. A separate worker process pulls jobs off the queue and runs an 11-step LLM-assisted pipeline that turns the raw message into:
- entities and facts in **Neo4j** (the temporal knowledge graph),
- embeddings of those entities and facts in **Pinecone** (the recall index),
- and a paper-trail of every step in **Postgres** (`processing_logs`).

Three datastores, two paths (a fast API path and a slow worker path), one queue between them. That's the entire system.

---

## 1. Storage topology — where each thing lives

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  POSTGRES (Neon)                                                         │
│   • users           — id, email, password_hash                           │
│   • sessions        — id, user_id, title       (groups messages)         │
│   • episodes        — id, user_id, session_id, actor, content,           │
│                       status, metadata (jsonb), occurred_at, processed_at│
│   • processing_logs — episode_id, step, status, duration_ms, message     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NEO4J (graph)                                                           │
│                                                                          │
│   (:Episode)──[:MENTIONS]──▶(:Entity)──[:FACT]──▶(:Entity)               │
│                                                                          │
│   :Entity   props: entityId, userId, name, normalizedName,               │
│                    type (PERSON/COMPANY/...), summary,                   │
│                    createdAt, updatedAt                                  │
│                                                                          │
│   :FACT     props: factId, relationType, factText,                       │
│                    validFrom, validUntil          ← event time   (T)     │
│                    tCreated, tExpired             ← txn time     (T')    │ NEW
│                    episodeIds: list                ← provenance          │ NEW
│                    confidence, lastSeenAt                                 │ NEW
│                                                                          │
│   :Episode  props: episodeId, userId, sessionId, actor, content,         │
│                    occurredAt, createdAt                                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PINECONE (index "zep")                                                  │
│   one index, type discriminator in metadata, always filter by userId     │
│                                                                          │
│   entity vector:                                                         │
│     id = entityId                                                        │
│     embedding of "${name}: ${summary}"                                   │
│     meta = { type:"entity", userId, neo4jId, name, entityType }          │
│                                                                          │
│   fact vector:                                                           │
│     id = factId                                                          │
│     embedding of factText                                                │
│     meta = { type:"fact", userId, neo4jId, sourceId, targetId,           │
│              relationType, factText,                                     │
│              validFromMs, isOpen, validUntilMs }   ← all new metadata    │ NEW
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  REDIS / BullMQ                                                          │
│   queue: "memory-ingestion"   job: { episodeId }                         │
│   attempts: 3, exponential backoff                                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Mental model: **Postgres is durability + audit. Neo4j is the truth-of-the-world graph with full temporal history. Pinecone is the recall index for free-text queries. Redis is patience.**

---

## 2. The fast path — `POST /ingest` returns in milliseconds

**File:** `server/src/controllers/ingest.controller.ts` → `services/episodes.service.ts`

When a Postman request comes in:

```http
POST /ingest
authorization: Bearer <JWT>
content-type: application/json

{
  "sessionId": "...",
  "actor": "user",
  "content": "I'm building an AI memory system at Grok.",
  "occurredAt": "2026-05-07T10:00:00.000Z",     ← optional, defaults to now
  "type": "message"                              ← optional, defaults to "message"  [Tier 1.3]
}
```

Sequence:
1. Auth middleware verifies the JWT and sets `userId` on the request context. (Tenancy is enforced everywhere from this single source.)
2. Controller validates the body with `ingestSchema` (zod).
3. `episodes.service.createEpisode`:
   - Confirms `sessionId` belongs to this user (SELECT on `sessions`). Returns 404 if not.
   - INSERTs an `episodes` row with `status="queued"`, `occurredAt`, `actor`, `content`, and `metadata={type}` if you passed a non-default type.
   - Enqueues a BullMQ job: `ingestionQueue.add("process-episode", { episodeId }, { jobId: episodeId })`.
     - `jobId: episodeId` makes the job idempotent — if the same episode is enqueued twice, BullMQ dedupes.
4. Controller responds `202 { episodeId, status: "queued" }`.

**Zero LLM calls happen here.** Total wall-clock latency: ~5 ms. The client immediately moves on; the heavy lifting starts in another process.

**Tier 1.3 change:** the `type` field on the request body. Accepted values `"message" | "text" | "json"`. Currently stored in `episodes.metadata.type`. The worker reads it but treats all three identically (the extraction prompts are tuned for messages — the paper restricts itself to messages too, so this is just forward-compatible).

---

## 3. The worker — boots once, runs forever

**File:** `server/src/workers/ingestion.worker.ts`

```ts
const worker = new Worker<IngestionJobData>(
  INGESTION_QUEUE_NAME,
  processEpisode,
  { connection: redisConnection, concurrency: 4 },
);
```

A separate Bun process. Listens to the `memory-ingestion` queue. Concurrency 4 — up to 4 episodes processed in parallel. On final failure (attempts exhausted), it sets `episodes.status="failed"`.

Every step in `processEpisode` is wrapped by a `step(episodeId, name, fn)` helper that writes a row to `processing_logs` with the duration in milliseconds and the status. If you ever wonder where time went on an episode, just query `processing_logs WHERE episode_id = …`.

The pipeline runs 11 steps. I'll walk each in order.

---

## 4. Step 1 — mark "processing"

```ts
await db.update(episodes).set({ status: "processing" }).where(eq(episodes.id, episodeId));
```

A simple Postgres UPDATE. Lets any external observer see the episode is in flight.

---

## 5. Step 2 — fetch episode + recent context (`fetch_episode`, `fetch_recent`)

```ts
const episode = await fetchEpisodeById(episodeId);
const recent  = await getRecentMessages(episode.sessionId, 6, episodeId);
```

Two Postgres reads. `recent` is the last 6 messages from the same session in chronological order, excluding the current one. These 6 messages become the context every downstream LLM call sees, so the model can resolve pronouns ("he", "the project") and understand the conversational arc.

> The paper uses `n = 4` (two turns). Code uses 6. Not a meaningful difference for our purposes.

---

## 6. Step 3 — entity extraction (`extract_entities`)

**File:** `services/extraction.service.ts` → `extractEntities()`

One OpenAI call with `gpt-4o-mini`, JSON-mode response. The prompt says:
- Extract every named thing AND every concept the speaker has a relationship to.
- Use canonical full names (`"Apple Inc."` not `"apple"`).
- Multi-word noun phrases are SINGLE entities (`"AI memory system"`, not split).
- Resolve shortened references (`"the project"`) using `recent` context.
- **Never extract the speaker themselves** — they're tracked separately.
- Output: `{ entities: [{ name, type, summary }] }` where `type ∈ {PERSON, COMPANY, LOCATION, CONCEPT, PRODUCT, DATE, OTHER}`.

For `"I love dogs. I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco."` you'd see:
```
Dogs[CONCEPT], AI memory system[CONCEPT], Grok[COMPANY], San Francisco[LOCATION]
```

### 🆕 Tier 2.6 — Reflexion pass (flag `ZEP_LLM_REFLEXION=1`)

After the first extraction, an optional critic LLM call reviews the draft and returns `{ add: [...], remove: [...] }`. We filter out `remove` entries and append any `add` entries the draft missed.

**Why:** the paper calls this out (§2.2.1, "we employ a reflection technique inspired by reflexion"). A second pass catches subtle entities the first pass misses (`"I've been getting into bouldering"` — first pass might skip "Bouldering"; critic catches it) AND drops hallucinated ones.

**Fails safe:** if the critic LLM errors or returns malformed JSON, the function logs and returns the unmodified draft.

---

## 7. Step 4 — entity resolution (`resolve_entities`)

**File:** `services/resolver.service.ts` → `resolveEntities()`

This step decides for each extracted entity: is this a brand-new thing, or one we already know about? Two paths, both gated by user.

### Always-on: normalized-name match

```ts
normalizedName = name.trim().toLowerCase().replace(/\s+/g, ' ')
const existing = await findEntitiesByNormalizedNames(userId, normalizedNames)
```

If a row comes back → reuse existing `entityId`, mark `isNew: false`. If not → mint a fresh UUID, `isNew: true`, and INSERT into Neo4j via `upsertEntities`.

### 🆕 Tier 2.7 — Hybrid semantic resolution (flag `ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1`)

For entities that did NOT match by normalized name, run a semantic lookup BEFORE creating a new node:
1. `embed(name + ": " + summary)` via OpenAI.
2. Query Pinecone with `filter: { userId, type: "entity" }`, topK=5, score ≥ 0.55.
3. `findEntitiesByIds` pulls the full Neo4j props for those candidate IDs.
4. **Type filter** — drop candidates whose `type` differs. A PERSON named "Apple" never merges with the COMPANY "Apple".
5. LLM resolution prompt (paper Appendix 6.1.2) judges: `{ is_duplicate: bool, existingEntityId, canonicalName }`.
6. If `is_duplicate: true` with a valid existing ID → reuse it; optionally update the entity's name to the canonical form.

**Why:** "Apple" and "Apple Inc." stop becoming two separate entities.

**Fails safe:** if any step errors, falls through to the original create-new branch.

The output of Step 4 is `resolved: ResolvedEntity[]`, each with `{ entityId, name, type, summary, isNew, ... }`. Only entries with `isNew: true` will be pushed to Pinecone in Step 8.

---

## 8. Step 5 — ensure the speaker is registered (`get_self_entity`)

```ts
const self = await getOrCreateSelfEntity(episode.userId)
```

Idempotently upserts a `User` entity whose `entityId = userId`. Always exists for every authenticated user. **Never sent to Pinecone** — it's intentionally not vectorizable; queries find the user implicitly through facts that point to it.

`allEntities = [self, ...resolved]` becomes the entity universe for the next step.

---

## 9. Step 6 — fact extraction (`extract_facts`)

**File:** `services/extraction.service.ts` → `extractFacts()`

Another OpenAI call. Prompt lists every entity (including `User`) and asks for two arrays:

```json
{
  "facts": [
    { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e1",
      "relationType": "LOVES", "factText": "The user loves dogs.",
      "confidence": 0.99 }
  ],
  "invalidations": [
    { "sourceEntityId": "u1", "targetEntityId": "e2", "relationType": "BUILDING" }
  ]
}
```

Key prompt rules:
- `relationType` is `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`. zod regex enforces it.
- Both source and target must be in the supplied entity list.
- No self-loops.
- **Negation handling is explicit**: `"I left Grok"` does NOT produce a positive fact `LEFT_AT`; it produces an `invalidation` for `WORKS_AT`. This separation is what makes downstream contradiction detection clean.

The post-LLM code defensively filters both arrays to drop any unknown entity IDs or self-loops.

---

## 10. Step 7 — temporal application (`apply_temporal`) — the heart of the system

**File:** `services/temporal.service.ts` → `applyTemporalFacts(facts, invalidations, episodeId, occurredAt, ctx)`

This is the most complex step. It runs in five sub-phases.

### 7a. 🆕 Tier 2.5 — LLM temporal extraction (flag `ZEP_LLM_TEMPORAL=1`)

Before any writes happen, for each new fact in parallel:
```ts
const t = await extractTemporal(fact.factText, ctx.current, ctx.recent, occurredAt)
// → { validAt: string | null, invalidAt: string | null }
```

The prompt (paper Appendix 6.1.5, with our hardening) parses absolute dates (`"June 23, 1912"`), relative dates (`"two years ago"`, `"yesterday"`), and present-tense (`"now"`) — and respects a critical hardening rule: `invalidAt` must be strictly later than `validAt`, otherwise both must be null.

**Why this hardening:** during scenario testing we found the LLM would mis-handle `"I love cats now, not dogs"` and emit an `invalidAt` before `validAt`, producing a closed-on-arrival edge. Two defenses prevent it now:
- The prompt itself includes rules 9–11: "now" alone means present tense; `invalidAt > validAt` strictly; contrastive negation ("not dogs") refers to OTHER subjects.
- A code-level guard in `temporal.service.ts`: if `until <= from`, drop `until` regardless of what the LLM returned.

Results land in `temporalByIndex: Map<i, {validAt, invalidAt}>` and are consumed in 7c below.

### 7b. Rule-based contradiction closure

For each new fact, look up existing OPEN edges on the same `(source, relationType)`. If the existing edge's target ≠ the new fact's target, close the existing edge:

```cypher
MATCH ()-[f:FACT { factId: $factId }]->()
SET f.validUntil = datetime($validUntil),
    f.tExpired   = coalesce(f.tExpired, datetime())   ← Tier 1.1
```

**🆕 Tier 1.1 change:** `closeFact` also stamps `tExpired = now()`. This is paper's *transaction time* — when the system *learned* the fact ended, distinct from `validUntil` which is event time (when it ended in the real world). The `coalesce` makes re-closes a no-op (idempotent).

Example: existing `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Grok` open. New fact `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Anthropic`. The Grok edge gets `validUntil = occurredAt` AND `tExpired = now()`. The edge is **preserved**, not deleted — that's the temporal-graph property that lets retrieval answer "what did the user believe in March?" vs "what's true now?".

### 7c. Write each new fact — three branches

Iterate the `rawFacts`. For each, pick exactly one path:

**Branch A — Reaffirmation (always on).** If an open edge already exists with the exact same `(source, relationType, target)` triple, treat this as the same fact stated again.

```ts
if (reaffirmation) {
  writes.push(reinforceFact(reaffirmation.factId, episodeId, occurredAt));
  skipped++;
  continue;
}
```

**🆕 Tier 1.2 change:** This used to be a silent `skipped++` and the information was lost. Now `reinforceFact` runs an idempotent Cypher statement that:
1. Appends `episodeId` to the edge's `episodeIds` list (skip if already present — idempotent).
2. Bumps `confidence` by 0.01, capped at 1.0.
3. Updates `lastSeenAt = datetime(occurredAt)`.

This is paper's "bidirectional indices that track the relationships between edges and their source episodes" (§2.1). Now you can cite every supporting message for a fact, and repeat assertions strengthen confidence instead of disappearing.

**🆕 Branch B — Tier 2.9 pair-scoped semantic dedup (flag `ZEP_LLM_FACT_DEDUP=1`).** Before writing, scan all existing OPEN edges between the same `(source, target)` pair. Embed each `factText`. If cosine similarity ≥ 0.85, treat as reaffirmation of THAT edge — call `reinforceFact` on it, skip the new edge.

**Why:** the LLM might produce `WORKS_AT` in one message and `EMPLOYED_BY` in another for the same underlying fact. Branch A misses it (different relationType). Branch B catches the semantic equivalence.

**Branch C — Write a new edge (default).** Mint a fresh `factId = randomUUID()`, build a `GraphFact`, call `upsertFact`. The Cypher writes:

```cypher
MATCH (a:Entity { entityId: $sourceEntityId })
MATCH (b:Entity { entityId: $targetEntityId })
MERGE (a)-[f:FACT { factId: $factId }]->(b)
ON CREATE SET
  f.relationType = $relationType,
  f.factText     = $factText,
  f.validFrom    = datetime($validFrom),
  f.validUntil   = CASE WHEN $validUntil IS NULL THEN NULL ELSE datetime($validUntil) END,
  f.confidence   = $confidence,
  f.episodeId    = $episodeId,
  f.episodeIds   = [$episodeId],        ← Tier 1.2
  f.createdAt    = datetime(),
  f.tCreated     = datetime(),          ← Tier 1.1
  f.lastSeenAt   = datetime($validFrom)
```

`validFrom` is the LLM-derived `validAt` if Tier 2.5 was on and produced one; otherwise `occurredAt`. `validUntil` is the LLM-derived `invalidAt` after the degenerate-guard (7a above), or null.

### 7d. Explicit invalidations

For each entry in `invalidations[]` from Step 6, find open edges matching `(source, relationType, target)` and close them:

```ts
await closeFact(m.factId, occurredAt)
```

Same `closeFact` as in 7b — sets `validUntil` AND `tExpired`. This is what closes `User-[BUILDING]-AI memory system` when message 5 says "I'm not building the memory system anymore."

### 7e. 🆕 Tier 2.8 — Semantic edge invalidation (flag `ZEP_LLM_INVALIDATE=1`)

After all the deterministic closures, if any rawFacts exist, run an LLM-driven contradiction sweep:

1. Collect every open edge on each source entity, EXCEPT ones already closed in 7b/7d AND except exact `(source, relationType, target)` matches with the new fact.
2. Embed the new fact's text AND each candidate's `factText`. Cosine-rank.
3. Filter similarity ≥ 0.45, top-5.
4. For each, call `llmJudgeContradiction(newText, existingText)` — a focused prompt that returns `{ is_contradiction: bool }`. The prompt has examples:
   - `"User lives in Boston"` vs `"User lives in Tokyo"` → contradicts.
   - `"User likes dogs"` vs `"User likes cats"` → NOT a contradiction (both can be true).
5. On `true`, `closeFact(open.factId, occurredAt)`.

**Why:** the rule path in 7b only catches exact-predicate contradictions. `LIVES_IN Boston` vs new `RESIDES_IN Tokyo` slips through. The semantic sweep catches it via embedding similarity + LLM judgement.

**Fails safe:** can only close *more* than the baseline; if the LLM judge errors, returns false, or the embedding fails, nothing closes.

### Return shape

```ts
{
  written: TemporalFact[],     // new edges, used to populate Pinecone
  closed: ClosedEdge[],         // every fact closed in 7b + 7d + 7e
  skipped: number               // reaffirmations + dedup merges
}
```

The worker logs:
```
[abc123de] temporal: written=4 closed=1 skipped=0
```

---

## 11. Step 8a — entity vectors to Pinecone (`upsert_entity_vectors`)

**File:** `services/pinecone.service.ts` → `upsertEntityVectors(resolved)`

```ts
const newOnes = entities.filter((e) => e.isNew);
if (newOnes.length === 0) return 0;
const vectors = await embedMany(newOnes.map(e => `${e.name}: ${e.summary}`));
await pineconeIndex.upsert({ records });
```

Only `isNew: true` entities get embedded — re-mentioning "Grok" never produces a new vector. Metadata: `{ type:"entity", userId, neo4jId, name, entityType }`.

**Exception:** the `User`/self entity is never pushed here. `resolved` doesn't include it; only `[self, ...resolved]` does, and we explicitly pass just `resolved`. The User is matched implicitly via fact vectors pointing to it.

---

## 12. Step 8b — fact vectors to Pinecone (`upsert_fact_vectors`)

```ts
const vectors = await embedMany(facts.map(f => f.factText));
await pineconeIndex.upsert({ records });
```

Every fact in `temporal.written` gets embedded (reaffirmations + dedup merges are never in `written`, so they don't generate new vectors).

**🆕 Tier 1.4 — Mirror temporal data on fact metadata:**
```ts
metadata: {
  type: "fact",
  userId,
  neo4jId: f.factId,
  sourceId, targetId, relationType, factText,
  validFromMs: new Date(f.validFrom).getTime(),
  isOpen: validUntilMs === null,
  ...(validUntilMs !== null ? { validUntilMs } : {}),
}
```

This lets retrieval do coarse temporal pruning at the vector layer (`filter: { isOpen: true }` or `validFromMs: { $lte: asOfMs }`) without round-tripping to Neo4j for every candidate.

---

## 13. 🆕 Step 8c — propagate closures to Pinecone (`update_closed_fact_vectors`) — the latest fix

**File:** `services/pinecone.service.ts` → `markFactVectorsClosed(temporal.closed)`

```ts
await pineconeIndex.update({
  id: c.factId,
  metadata: { isOpen: false, validUntilMs },
});
```

For every fact that 7b / 7d / 7e closed, push a **metadata-only update** to Pinecone. No re-embedding cost (the vector content doesn't change). `isOpen` flips to `false`, `validUntilMs` is set to the `closedAt` timestamp.

**Why this matters:** before this step existed, Pinecone metadata could drift forever — closing an edge in Neo4j left the Pinecone vector with `isOpen: true` indefinitely. That made the `isOpen` filter unusable for retrieval, defeating the purpose of Tier 1.4. Now Pinecone always reflects Neo4j's truth.

**Fails safe:** wrapped in try/catch inside `markFactVectorsClosed`. If Pinecone hiccups, we log per-factId and move on. Neo4j is authoritative; Pinecone's job is recall, and a stale-but-correct vector is recoverable on next ingest.

Worker log line now reads:
```
[abc123de] pinecone: 4 entity vector(s), 4 fact vector(s) upserted, 1 closed
```

---

## 14. Step 9 — episode node + mentions edges (`create_episode_node`, `link_episode_entities`)

**File:** `services/neo4j.service.ts`

```cypher
MERGE (e:Episode { episodeId: $episodeId })
ON CREATE SET ...

MATCH (ep:Episode { episodeId: $episodeId })
UNWIND $entityIds AS eid
MATCH (ent:Entity { entityId: eid })
MERGE (ep)-[m:MENTIONS]->(ent)
ON CREATE SET m.timestamp = datetime($occurredAt)
```

Creates `:Episode` for the raw message and a `:MENTIONS` edge to every entity in `[self, ...resolved]`. This is how you trace any fact or entity back to the messages that produced it — paper §2.1's "non-lossy data store from which entities and relations are extracted."

In a future change (not done yet), we could also push the episode ID onto each new fact edge's `episodeIds`, giving us bidirectional traceability. Currently the link is via `episodeIds` on the FACT only.

---

## 15. Step 10 — done

```ts
await db.update(episodes)
  .set({ status: "processed", processedAt: new Date() })
  .where(eq(episodes.id, episodeId));
```

Mark Postgres `processed`, log total wall-clock time. Worker emits a `completed` event. The episode is now fully queryable.

---

## 16. Per-step timing in practice

From a real worker log (yours, lightly):
```
fetch_episode               63 ms
fetch_recent                59 ms
extract_entities          3908 ms   ← LLM, biggest hit
resolve_entities          3234 ms   ← LLM (only with ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1)
get_self_entity             71 ms
extract_facts             1975 ms   ← LLM
apply_temporal              91 ms   ← can be 5-15s with all flags on
upsert_entity_vectors      561 ms   ← OpenAI embeddings + Pinecone upsert
upsert_fact_vectors          0 ms   ← in this run, no new facts
update_closed_fact_vectors   0 ms   ← in this run, no closures
create_episode_node         91 ms
link_episode_entities      106 ms
```

Total ~10–30 seconds with all flags on. Baseline (no flags) ~5–10 seconds.

---

## 17. Tier 1 changes (always on) — summary

| ID | Change | File | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.1** | `tCreated` on insert, `tExpired` on close | `neo4j.service.ts` | Paper's transaction-time axis. Lets you reconstruct "what the system believed at time T". |
| **1.2** | `episodeIds: list` instead of scalar `episodeId`. `reinforceFact` on reaffirmation. | `neo4j.service.ts`, `temporal.service.ts` | Multi-episode provenance + confidence reinforcement on repeat assertions. |
| **1.3** | `type: "message" \| "text" \| "json"` on ingest | `ingest.validator.ts`, `episodes.service.ts` | API matches paper §2.1. Stored in `metadata` jsonb — no schema migration. |
| **1.4** | Mirror `validFromMs`, `validUntilMs`, `isOpen` on Pinecone fact metadata | `pinecone.service.ts` | Coarse temporal pruning at the vector layer. |
| **1.5** | (new) Pinecone closure-update step | `pinecone.service.ts`, `ingestion.worker.ts` | Keeps Pinecone metadata in sync with Neo4j when edges get closed. |

---

## 18. Tier 2 changes (flag-gated) — summary

All default OFF. Each enabled with `ZEP_LLM_…=1` in `.env.local`. Each fails safe — if the LLM call errors, the path falls back to the baseline outcome.

| Flag | Stage hit | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `ZEP_LLM_REFLEXION=1` | Step 3 (entity extraction) | Critic pass adds missed entities, removes hallucinations. |
| `ZEP_LLM_ENTITY_RESOLVE=1` | Step 4 (entity resolution) | Cosine + LLM dedup for "Apple" vs "Apple Inc." cases. |
| `ZEP_LLM_TEMPORAL=1` | Step 7a (inside apply_temporal) | Resolves relative dates: "two years ago" → actual datetime. Now has hardened prompt + degenerate-guard. |
| `ZEP_LLM_INVALIDATE=1` | Step 7e (inside apply_temporal) | Semantic contradiction detection: `LIVES_IN Boston` vs `RESIDES_IN Tokyo`. |
| `ZEP_LLM_FACT_DEDUP=1` | Step 7c branch B (inside apply_temporal) | Merges `WORKS_AT` and `EMPLOYED_BY` for same source-target into a single edge. |

---

## 19. How to verify ingestion end-to-end

From Postman or the shell:

**Watch one episode finish:**
```http
GET /episodes/:id
```
Returns `episode.status` and `logs[]` — every step with its duration and any error.

**See the whole graph for a user:**
```http
GET /graph
```
Returns `entities[]`, `facts[]` (with timestamps), `episodes[]`, `mentions[]`.

**Query the memory:**
```http
POST /retrieve
{ "query": "...", "sessionId": "...", "limit": 6 }
```
Returns `answer` (LLM synthesized), `facts` (top-ranked), `context` (formatted), `recentMessages`.

**CLI verification:**
```bash
bun run verify          # walks PG + Neo4j + Pinecone for most-recent user
bun run test:temporal   # 21 in-process assertions on Tier 1 paths
bun run test:tier2      # 22 assertions across all five Tier 2 flags
bun run scenario        # end-to-end 5-message scenario via HTTP
```

---

## 20. Known limitations / what's not yet done

1. **Worker concurrency race.** With `concurrency: 4`, two episodes can race — we saw the Cats/Dogs ordering bug. The cheapest fix is a one-line Cypher guard in `closeFact`: `WHERE f.validFrom <= datetime($validUntil)` to refuse backwards-in-time closures. Or set `concurrency: 1`. Both are pending decisions.
2. **Community subgraph.** Paper §2.3 specifies a third subgraph layer with label-propagation clustering and community summaries. Not implemented; a separate worker and queue would be needed.
3. **BFS retrieval lane.** Paper §3.1 has three search functions (BM25, cosine, BFS). We have cosine only.
4. **Embedding model.** Paper uses BGE-m3 (1024-d). We use `text-embedding-3-small` (1536-d). Functionally fine; not paper-identical.
5. **Bidirectional episode index on facts.** We have `:MENTIONS` from episodes to entities, but the supporting episodes on a fact are tracked only via the `episodeIds` array; there's no separate `:SUPPORTS` edge. Mostly stylistic.

---

## 21. The whole pipeline in one runnable sentence

> A POST inserts an episode row, enqueues a job, and returns 202. A worker process picks up the job, marks the row processing, loads the last 6 session messages, asks an LLM to extract entities, optionally critics them, deduplicates them against Neo4j by normalized name (and optionally by Pinecone+LLM), ensures the User entity exists, asks an LLM to extract facts and explicit invalidations between those entities, optionally derives event-time bounds for each fact with another LLM call (rejecting degenerate results), closes any open edges that contradict the new facts (rule-based + optionally semantic), writes the survivors as new `:FACT` edges with `tCreated` and `episodeIds`, embeds each new entity and each new fact and upserts them to Pinecone (with `isOpen`/`validFromMs` metadata), patches the metadata of just-closed fact vectors so Pinecone reflects the closures, creates an `:Episode` node, links it to every mentioned entity, marks the row processed, and emits a one-line summary log.

That's the whole pipeline. Every word above maps to a step you can find in `workers/ingestion.worker.ts` calling into the services in `services/*.ts`.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:24:04Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 16:24:16Z)**_

Almost — close, but I'd refine the phrasing slightly. It's:

> **One vector per new entity** (about the THING) + **one vector per new fact edge** (about the RELATIONSHIP).

You're seeing both types side by side because they describe **different things about the same world**:

```
ENTITY vector:  "AI memory system" — what this thing IS
   id:  59ca1fbd...  (= the entityId)
   embedded text:  "AI memory system: <one-line summary>"
   answers queries like: "tell me about the memory system"
   
FACT vector:    "The user is building an AI memory system." — a relationship INVOLVING that thing
   id:  a903ecb0...  (= the factId)
   embedded text:  "The user is building an AI memory system."
   sourceId: c9a21c02...   ← User entityId
   targetId: 59ca1fbd...   ← matches the entity vector above
   answers queries like: "what is the user building"
```

Notice the **link** between the two: the fact vector's `targetId` (`59ca1fbd…`) IS the entity vector's `_id` (`59ca1fbd…`). Same ID — that's how the graph stitches the two layers together. Retrieval can land on either vector type and hop to the other via Neo4j using these IDs.

## The exact rule, in one table

| Triggers a vector? | When |
|---|---|
| ✅ Entity mentioned for the FIRST time (new for this user) | One entity vector created |
| ❌ Entity mentioned again later | No new vector — reuse existing |
| ✅ Brand-new `:FACT` edge written to Neo4j | One fact vector created |
| ❌ Same fact re-asserted (reaffirmation) | No new vector — existing edge's `episodeIds` grows |
| ❌ Pair-scoped semantic merge (Tier 2.9) | No new vector — existing edge reinforced |
| ❌ Edge closed | No new vector — existing one's metadata patched (isOpen→false) |

## Your 8 vectors, accounted for

From your message `"I love dogs. I'm building an AI memory system at Grok in San Francisco."`:

**4 entities** (each new, each gets one entity vector):
- `Dogs` [CONCEPT]
- `AI memory system` [CONCEPT]
- `Grok` [COMPANY]
- `San Francisco` [LOCATION]

**4 facts** (each is a new `:FACT` edge, each gets one fact vector):
- `User -[LOVES]-> Dogs` → "The user loves dogs."
- `User -[BUILDING]-> AI memory system` → "The user is building an AI memory system."
- `User -[WORKS_AT]-> Grok` → "The user works at Grok."
- `Grok -[LOCATED_IN]-> San Francisco` → "Grok is located in San Francisco."

= **8 vectors total.** Matches your Pinecone screenshot.

## Important asymmetry

The `User` entity does NOT get its own vector — that's the one entity we skip on purpose. So if you have N entities in Neo4j for a user, you have **N − 1** entity vectors in Pinecone (the User is the missing one). Facts that involve the User still get vectors as normal because the fact vector is keyed by the RELATIONSHIP, not the User.

So your understanding is correct in spirit:
- ✅ Every new entity → 1 entity vector
- ✅ Every new fact (= every new entity relationship) → 1 fact vector
- ❗ One asterisk: the `User` entity itself never gets a vector

Yes, you've got it.
